issue_title: YAML defaults cannot be overridden by test specifics
issue_body:
Feature request? It would be lovely if a test-specific could override the defaults' value.

Example:
```yaml
defaults:
    request_headers:
        accept: application/json
        content-type: application/json
    response_headers:
        content-type: application/json; charset=utf-8

<-- snip -->

tests:
    - name: delete note test case
      verbose: True
      method: DELETE
      url: /v2/notes/$RESPONSE['$.id']
      request_headers:
          accept: text/javascript
          content-type: text/javascript; charset=utf-8
      response_headers:
          content-type: text/javascript; charset=utf-8
      status: 302 || 200
```

This causes "delete note test case" to return application/json. 

Workaround: If I nix the defaults and specify content-type and accept header details for each test, all is well.

issue_title: when --debug is passed, epoll cannot be found
issue_body:
This is issue [#413 on Flask-SocketIO](https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/Flask-SocketIO/issues/413). When using `gevent` as the async mode for SocketIO and with `debug` set to `True`, it monkey patches the `select` module and removes, among other things, `select.epoll` and `select.epoll.poll`. This results in an unusable app. Again this only applies to gdbgui when the `--debug` flag is passed.

This issue was created to document the behavior, and will be fixed in a future version. Current workaround if you are developing and running in debug mode is to change `async_mode='gevent'` to `async_mode='eventlet'` (and to `pip install eventlet`).

issue_body:
A number of the test fixtures that generate data are powered by `os.urandom`. This works fine until it generates a random sequence of bytes that starts with a leading zero. This will cause tests to fail during duration due to [int.bit_length](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#int.bit_length) stripping leading zeros in its computation.

Example test failure: https://travis-ci.org/ahawker/ulid/jobs/294263189

All of the above is a side-effect of the fact that there is no validation logic for the `timestamp` portion of a `ULID`. It should never contain a zero leading byte since the minimum value is the Unix epoch.

Items to address this issue:

* Validation rules to enforce minimum and maximum `timestamp` values upon creation
* Update test fixtures to specific generates values within valid or invalid ranges

Example:

```python
>>> import ulid
>>> data = b"\x00\xcdh\x95}\xd9\xb2Yp':y0\xe4\xce\xdc"
>>> ulid.from_bytes(data)
<ULID('00SNM9AZESP9CQ09STF4RE9KPW')>
>>> ulid.from_int(int.from_bytes(data, byteorder='big'))
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/hawker/src/github.com/ahawker/ulid/ulid/api.py", line 76, in from_int
    raise ValueError('Expects integer to be 128 bits; got {} bytes'.format(length))
ValueError: Expects integer to be 128 bits; got 15 bytes
```

issue_title: Additional options for use with spack
issue_body:
Hi Stefano. As I've been writing my own scientific Fortran software as part of my degree, the need to manage dependencies has become increasingly important. As such, I've finally turned my attention to a package manager again. I've been looking at [spack](http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html), which should allow for easy dependency resolution and a simple build process for the end user. Rather than have to mess around with CMake, it would be nice to be able to use FoBiS with spack, but there are a few small issues which I foresee there.

The most important is that spack specifies the compiler via environment variables (`FC` for modern Fortran). It would be great if there was a way to get FoBiS.py to read the compiler from there. Another useful feature would be the existence of an `install` command. I suppose this could be achieved with a job defined in the `fobos` file, but that requires package maintainers to remember to write it. This command would need to have some way to specify the install-prefix.

These aren't absolutely vital--spack has enough features to be able to make FoBiS work as it exists now. But they would make life easier and reduce the overhead needed whenever a FoBiS-built project is packaged for spack.

  File "/some_path/my_new_env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/unittest2/loader.py", line 395, in _get_module_from_name
    __import__(name)
  File "/home/josay/Geekage/six_bugs/bug.py", line 8, in <module>
    hasattr(o, 'toto')
  File "/some_path/my_new_env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/six.py", line 117, in __getattr__
    _module = self._resolve()
  File "/some_path/my_new_env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/six.py", line 114, in _resolve
    return _import_module(self.mod)
  File "/some_path/my_new_env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/six.py", line 81, in _import_module
    __import__(name)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/tkinter/__init__.py", line 38, in <module>
    raise ImportError(str(msg) + ', please install the python3-tk package')
ImportError: No module named '_tkinter', please install the python3-tk package
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 1 test in 0.001s

FAILED (errors=1)
```

runnning the following command

`python -m unittest2 discover --start-directory=. --pattern=*.py`

in a Python 3.5 virtual env on the following code:

```
import gc
objs = gc.get_objects()
for o in objs:
    hasattr(o, 'toto')
```

(Using unittest2 is required to ensure that `six` gets somehow loaded automatically. Just running the code directly leads to no error.)

**Details**

The `MovedModule.__getattr__` method may raise an ImportError .

**Expectation**

From https://docs.python.org/2/reference/datamodel.html#object.__getattr__ : 

> Called when an attribute lookup has not found the attribute in the usual places (i.e. it is not an instance attribute nor is it found in the class tree for self). name is the attribute name. This method should return the (computed) attribute value or raise an AttributeError exception.

Thus, I'd expect an AttributeError to be raised when the import in __getattr__ fails. Then it would be caught by `hasattr` or `getattr` with a default value.

**Potential fix**

Rewriting the method likes this seems to fix the problem:

```
   def __getattr__(self, attr):
        try:
            _module = self._resolve()
        except ImportError:
            raise AttributeError("ImportError during __getattr__")
        value = getattr(_module, attr)
        setattr(self, attr, value)
        return value

```
I'm happy to submit a pull-request if you think it makes sense.

issue_title: multiple subscriptions slows down characteristic throughput
issue_body:
### TLDR: Does anyone have an idea how to subscribe to multiple notifications using PyGATT without significant packet loss?

I have been having an issue with some BLE interfacing code for some while now. I am using the PyGATT module in Python 3 on a Linux machine to receive data from a Bluetooth LE device (MyoArmband from Thalmic Labs). For some reason, I am not able to get the advertised sampling rate for any of the data streams (EMG and IMU). I can connect and set up the device fine, however, my computed sampling rates are much slower than they should be.

For example, I should be getting a data rate of 200 Hz for EMG and 50 Hz for IMU, however, with this code I am only getting ~70 Hz from EMG and ~17 Hz IMU. I've tested a few configurations and determined that if I only subscribe to a single characteristic at a time, I can get the correct sampling rate for that characteristic. This suggests that my code must be dropping packets only when multiple characteristics are subscribed to. Is this a known issue with PyGATT / is there any way around this? If not with PyGATT, does anyone have a suggestion for another module that I can use in Python for interfacing with BLE devices (preferably cross-platform)?

Note: I've attached my code for reference.
`
import pygatt
import time
import numpy as np

emgcount = 0
imucount = 0

def emg_handler(handle, value): 
    global emgcount 
    emgcount += 2

def imu_handler(handle, value): 
    global imucount 
    imucount += 1

def main():
   btle = pygatt.BGAPIBackend()

    btle.start()
    myo = btle.connect( 'e0:f2:99:e7:60:40' )

    try:
        # set parameters
        myo.char_write('d5060401-a904-deb9-4748-2c7f4a124842', b'\x01\x03\x00\x00\x00')   # deregister all streaming
        myo.char_write('d5060401-a904-deb9-4748-2c7f4a124842', b'\x0a\x01\x02')           # lock myo
        myo.char_write('d5060401-a904-deb9-4748-2c7f4a124842', b'\x09\x01\x01')           # don't sleep
        myo.char_write('d5060401-a904-deb9-4748-2c7f4a124842', b'\x01\x03\x02\x01\x00')   # stream filtered emg and imu

        # subscribe to everything
        myo.subscribe('d5060402-a904-deb9-4748-2c7f4a124842', callback = imu_handler)
        myo.subscribe('d5060105-a904-deb9-4748-2c7f4a124842', callback = emg_handler)
        myo.subscribe('d5060205-a904-deb9-4748-2c7f4a124842', callback = emg_handler)
        myo.subscribe('d5060305-a904-deb9-4748-2c7f4a124842', callback = emg_handler)
        myo.subscribe('d5060405-a904-deb9-4748-2c7f4a124842', callback = emg_handler)

        print( 'Streaming...' )
        t0 = time.time()
        while ( time.time() - t0 ) < 10.0: 
            time.sleep( 1.0 )

    finally:
        tf = time.time()
        btle.stop()

        print('')
        print( 'EMG SAMPLING RATE:', emgcount / ( tf - t0 ) ) # should be ~200, is returning ~70
        print( 'IMU SAMPLING RATE:', imucount / ( tf - t0 ) ) # should be ~50, is returning ~17

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
`

issue_title: Generators for attribute values not accepted by Connection.modify()
issue_body:
I'm generating data for my database using generators but at the point it's being passed back to the database it's being interpreted incorrectly. I'm more or less doing:

```python
new_values = (i for i in range(3))
changes = {'testAttribute' : new_values}
connection.modify('ou=test,dc=example,dc=com', changes)
```

which ends up writing something like (retrieved using `ldapsearch` on the command line):

    dn: ou=test,dc=example,dc=com
    testAttribute:: PGdlbmVyYXRvciBvYmplY3QgPGdlbmV4cHI+IGF0IDB4N2Y3YTQ0ZjQwYTY4Pg==

which is the base64 encoded version of:

    dn: ou=test,dc=example,dc=com
    testAttribute: <generator object <genexpr> at 0x7f7a44f40a68>

but I would expect:

    dn: ou=test,dc=example,dc=com
    testAttribute: 0
    testAttribute: 1
    testAttribute: 2

So it's writing the `__str__` version of the object rather than iterating its values. This seems to come down to the explicit type testing happening at https://github.com/cannatag/ldap3/blob/master/ldap3/operation/modify.py#L64 by testing against `ldap3.SEQUENCE_TYPES`. Perhaps it would be better to use `collections.abc.Iterable` with a special case to filter out string types?

I expect this applies to `Connection.add()` as well.

issue_title: Docs related to Server Pool seems outdated
issue_body:
http://ldap3.readthedocs.io/server.html?highlight=round#server-pool

Can't find POOLING_STRATEGY_ROUND_ROBIN

I have just been checking out yas3fs as I thought it might be useful in my application.

FYI: S3 and EC2 where I ran my tests are both in the same region.

The first test I did was to mount one of my buckets and then attempt to do an operation (sha1 in this case) on that file. The file was ~850MB in size and I was expecting there to be a delay on the first run because the file would need to be downloaded. That turned out to be the case, but wildly more than I was expecting.

```
$ time sha1sum 03302014-r1.nd.ome.tif
efebdfcd4241fb53fc8f04d31897970db21bdca1  03302014-r1.nd.ome.tif

real	2m9.312s
user	0m2.448s
sys	0m1.318s
```

I thought that was rather a long time as I can download this file from s3 to this EC2 instance in ~15s usually. Anyway, I thought I'd then repeat the experiment to see how it performed once the file has been cached locally. I was surprised that the result was not a lot better which I guess points to the overhead of yas3fs/FUSE as the problem.

```
$ time sha1sum 03302014-r1.nd.ome.tif
efebdfcd4241fb53fc8f04d31897970db21bdca1  03302014-r1.nd.ome.tif

real	1m40.551s
user	0m2.568s
sys	0m1.245s
```

Just in case something weird was happening in the case of calculating the sha1, I also tried a basic copy operation (to a non s3 location):

```
$ time cp 03302014-r1.nd.ome.tif ~/

real	1m37.469s
user	0m0.002s
sys	0m1.280s
```

Also for reference, this is how long it takes to calculate the sha1 from the cached file:

```
$ time sha1sum /tmp/yas3fs/dpwr/files/s3test/03302014-r1.nd.ome.tif
efebdfcd4241fb53fc8f04d31897970db21bdca1  /tmp/yas3fs/dpwr/files/s3test/03302014-r1.nd.ome.tif

real	0m2.890s
user	0m2.793s
sys	0m0.099s
```

Is this likely to be a bug or is that the expected performance of FUSE and/or S3 based filesystems? I don't have any previous experience with either and I'm trying to figure out the best avenue to explore for my application.

Thanks a lot,

Douglas

issue_title: Face Recognition Question about Deep Learning Model
issue_body:
Thank you @davisking for your great work.

I'm using Dlib's face recognition interface and it works well. I know @davisking  trained the `ResNet-34` model as wrote in this blog '[High Quality Face Recognition with Deep Metric Learning](http://blog.dlib.net/2017/02/high-quality-face-recognition-with-deep.html)', and my questions are:
1. Is only there one ResNet model (a 29-layers CNN model) in this face recognition model ? Are there some other neural network layers or models combined?
2. Which layers in ResNet-32 remain?
3. Which loss function used when training?
4. Any training details?

i trained resnet model using dlib_metric_learning_on_images. but when i'm using it for tests and try to detect any of image in johns directory it is unable to detect any person. however when i use trained model on bald_guys.jpg it detects them all as one person. what am i doing wrong while training?i also changed input_rgb_image_sized class of face_recognition to input_rgb_image class.

Hello,

to be honest, I'm not entirely sure if this is a problem with pysnmp, but using snmpwalk i got no problem. Here is the thing: I updated my server to Debian 9 today, and right after the upgrade, a python-script of mine using PySNMP stopped working:
```
#!/usr/bin/env python3

from pysnmp.entity.rfc3413.oneliner import cmdgen

#root_oid = (1, 3, 6, 1, 4, 1, 2021, 4)
root_oid = (1, 3, 6, 1, 4, 1, 2021, 10)

authentication = cmdgen.CommunityData("public")
transport = cmdgen.UdpTransportTarget(("172.22.120.3", 161))

errorIndication, errorStatus, errorIndex, varBinds = cmdgen.CommandGenerator().nextCmd(authentication, transport, root_oid)

if errorIndication:
    raise Exception(errorIndication)
if errorStatus:
    raise Exception(errorStatus.prettyPrint())

for varBind in varBinds:
    oid, value = varBind[0]
    print(oid)
```

结果：公司/nis, 的/ude1, **章有红/nr**, **章有/nr**, 黑/a, 章/q, ，/w, **都是/nr**, 对/p, **方会/nr**, 认可/vn, 的/ude1, 。/w

例3：那三年的年费能报销，这个季末测绘费能解决吗？
结果：**那三年/nr**, 的/ude1, **年费/nr**, 能/v, 报销/v, ，/w, 这个/rz, **季末/nr**, 测绘/vn, **费能/nr**, 解决/v, 吗/y, ？/w
例4：这张表到申报高新时才填。那还用加盖公章吗？
结果：这/rzv, **张表/nr**, 到/v, 申报/vn, **高新时/nr**, 才/d, 填/v, 。/w, **那还/nr**, 用/p, **加盖/nrf**, 公章/n, 吗/y, ？/w

See page 27 of docs:
https://www.bankid.com/assets/bankid/rp/riktlinjer-foer-mobilt-bankid-till-foerlitande-part-1-0-7.pdf

The docs don't explain it that well, but the collect response contains a signature from the client, and a OCSP response by what I'm guessing is the bank which signed the client's certificate. 

> The OCSP 0 response is signed by a certificate that has the same issuer as the certificate being 
> verified (i.e. the BankId).

I think the purpose for this is that it gives the bank power to revoke the user certificate. So the infrastructure in that sense is reliant on both the bank and the BankID backend. I think the bank controls the OCSP stapling, while BankID stands for the authentication infrastructure. 

I found some old documentation of the server architecture for the Norwegian system from 2005:
http://www.eurim.org.uk/activities/pi/BankIDWhitePaper.pdf

So there seems to be the root-CA which is jointly owned by all the banks. Then each bank gets its own level-1 CA signed by the root. 

Question is, do we need to verify these as a merchant? Why would they include them in the response otherwise?

issue_title: Keys are not visible in Seahorse
issue_body:
For testing, I created a dummy entry in the keyring.

```
import keyring
keyring.set_password('system', 'foo', 'bar')
```
Then the password can be retrieved using `keyring`:
```
keyring.set_password('system', 'foo')
```
but is not at all visible in Seahorse. In particular, no `'system'` keyring can be seen in Seahorse. Similarly, the key can't be looked up with `secret-tool`:
```
secret-tool lookup system foo
```
which yields nothing.

issue_title: Exception - TypeError: datetime.datetime(...) is not JSON serializable
issue_body:
Some API calls in boto3 include `datetime` objects in the dictionary return values. Datetime is not one of the types that the `json` package understands, causing an exception when it tries to serialize it into a JSON file for the `save()` method:

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/testcode", line 9, in <module>
    load_entry_point('testcode==0.1', 'console_scripts', 'testcode')()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/testcode-0.1-py2.7.egg/testcode/main.py", line 511, in main
    user.save_placebo()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/testcode-0.1-py2.7.egg/testcode/main.py", line 228, in save_placebo
    self.client.meta.placebo.save(path)
  File "build/bdist.macosx-10.10-x86_64/egg/placebo.py", line 60, in save
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/__init__.py", line 189, in dump
    for chunk in iterable:
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 434, in _iterencode
    for chunk in _iterencode_dict(o, _current_indent_level):
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 408, in _iterencode_dict
    for chunk in chunks:
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 408, in _iterencode_dict
    for chunk in chunks:
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 332, in _iterencode_list
    for chunk in chunks:
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 332, in _iterencode_list
    for chunk in chunks:
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 408, in _iterencode_dict
    for chunk in chunks:
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 408, in _iterencode_dict
    for chunk in chunks:
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 442, in _iterencode
    o = _default(o)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 184, in default
    raise TypeError(repr(o) + " is not JSON serializable")
TypeError: datetime.datetime(2015, 12, 3, 21, 20, 17, 326000, tzinfo=tzutc()) is not JSON serializable
```

The `json.dump()` method takes an optional argument `default`, allowing you to pass a function that returns serializable versions of objects. There's an example for datetime on Stack Overflow here:
http://stackoverflow.com/a/22238613

Similarly, the load() method will need to convert ISO 8601 strings back to `datetime` objects before using them for mocking. This could get tricky if someone uses timestamps for AWS object names, but maybe they could be marked for conversion. For example, `'placebo_timestamp:2015-12-3-21:20:17:326000Z'` is hopefully pretty unlikely as an AWS object name.

I will try to submit a PR for this soon.

issue_title: Inverted edges without a source variable
issue_body:
In #19, @danielhers comments:

> [...] I had an issue when trying to use penman to read AMRs in my branch. When reading `a_pmid_2094_2929.48` from the BioAMR training set (which contains the triple `f / figure~e.17 :mod "1A"~e.19)`, I was asking if `"1A" in amr.variables()` and the answer was `True`. I think this is because of the `:mod` edge, which is an inverted `:domain`. In a way, it is true that the triple `("1A", "domain", "f")` exists, but that doesn't mean `"1A"` is a variable, while `f` is.

This is due to the interaction of 2 assumptions I make in Penman:

* that `:mod` is the inverse of `:domain`
* that the `source` of a normalized (i.e., deinverted) triple will always be a node identifier

Specifically, the `AMRCodec` treats `mod` as the inversion of `domain`; that is, `mod` is considered an inverted edge while `domain` is the canonical form. The [guidelines for AMR](https://github.com/amrisi/amr-guidelines/blob/master/amr.md) suggest as much:

>We can write `:domain-of` as an inverse of `:domain`, but we often shorten this to `:mod`.

and

> ... we instead open up the inverse of `:domain` , i.e. the role `:mod`

On the other hand, `:mod` is in the table of relations with [reifications](https://github.com/amrisi/amr-guidelines/blob/master/amr.md#reification), while `:domain` is not, nor is `:domain` in the list of relations without reifications (either it was forgotten, or perhaps the author considered `:mod` to be the canonical form and `:domain` the inverse).

The second assumption with PENMAN is that the source of every non-inverted edge is a node identifier. If `mod` is not inverted, there is not really a problem: it's just an attribute triple like `:polarity` (assuming it's ok for `:mod` to behave that way). But does that mean that `:domain` cannot behave like an attribute? Or can both of them (meaning they are not simple inverses)?

I have an `numpy.ndarray` called `data` with 3 dimensions of sizes: `[N, M, L]`, and an appropriately chosen `LMAX`.  If I do transformations with slices along the first axis, everything works fine, i.e. 

Success:
```
spinsfast.map2salm(data[0, :, :], 1, LMAX)
```

If the array is reshaped to be `[M, L, N]` and I take slices along the last axis, the results are erroneous, i.e.

Failure:
```
spinsfast.map2salm(data[:, :, 0], 1, LMAX)
```

I realized (after a *long*) time that it could be a contiguous data issue, and indeed I was able to fix the problem by doing,

Success:
```
spinsfast.map2salm(np.copy(data[:, :, 0]), 1, LMAX)
```

I'm using the anaconda version (v104.1) which doesn't seem to have the wrapper functions you recently added.  I see that you also added a `np.ascontiguousarray` call; is that to fix this same issue?  If not, it shouldn't be hard for me to create a MWE if that's helpful.

From https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/master/psutil/tests/test_unicode.py:

```
On Python 3 the returned paths are supposed to match 100% (and this
is tested).
Not on Python 2 though, where we assume correct unicode path handling
is broken. In fact it is broken for most os.* functions, see:
http://bugs.python.org/issue18695
There really is no way for psutil to handle unicode correctly on
Python 2 unless we make such APIs return a unicode type in certain
circumstances.
I'd rather have unicode support broken on Python 2 than having APIs
returning variable str/unicode types, see:
https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/issues/655#issuecomment-136131180

As such we also test that all APIs on Python 2 always return str and
never unicode (in test_contracts.py).
```

Basically we assume unicode support is broken on Python 2 and want to make sure unicode is never returned. I added some tests about this (see: https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/commit/35ebdfb172f4d27678c6d33d1a6f74ca2771e303) already.
The only place were we may want to return unicode is `WindowsService.description()` so this serves as a reminder for that (needs investigation).

Also, I'm adding tests to make sure the same types (int, float, whatever..., see https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/commit/d67154d8a067526eaff953ae95561380b8f9b62a, https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/commit/5aa3af31aa35d904d358171ad1049b46205494cf and [test_contracts.py](https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/master/psutil/tests/test_contracts.py)) are returned across across platforms and I already bumped in a couple of errors on OSX but I expect there will be more.

issue_title: non unicode data not correctly handled in Python 3
issue_body: Filenames and command line arguments can include (non-unicode) binary data on most operating systems.  This is more difficult to handle on Python 3 because these are generally expressed as the str type.  I've created pull request https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/pull/733 with a test case that highlights the issue but do not have a solution at this point.

issue_title: Reconsider how handlers for message stanzas are registered

Possible solutions (just brainstorming here, comments welcome):

1. Add a special value referring to the clients own bare JID at any point in time, like what ``None`` currently does implicitly for IQs.

2. Make registration of wildcard handlers explicit and thus allow to register handlers for bare JIDs.

3. …?

issue_title: Add option to use original/alternative filename when guessing movie/episode properties
issue_body:
If I understood correctly, refiners will enhance the set of information of our video.
The initial info that exists is the video path. Using the video path:
- guessit will create the initial set of information from the file path / file name.
- the metadata refiner will try to get more details about the video (for mkv containers),
- then omdb will obtain more movie details
- and then tvdb will obtain more tv shows details

Even guessit (1st step) could have been a "refiner".

Each one organize movies and series in a different way, and most of the time, file renaming is used.
In my case, I usually keep some metadata files alongside the video file, including a file that contains the original filename of the video. Initially I wasn't keeping the quality, release group and other info in the filename:
```
Foo Bar/Season 01/Foo Bar S01E01 - Super Title.mkv
Foo Bar/Season 01/Foo Bar S01E01 - Super Title.meta
```
```
$> cat "Foo Bar/Season 01/Foo Bar S01E01 - Super Title.meta"
Foo.Bar.S01E01.Super.Title.720p.WEBRip.AAC2.0.H.264-RGROUP
```

It would be really nice to have an additional refiner that enhances the guessit properties of your video based on some additional file/information that's present.

This is just an idea and a use case. I believe that file renaming is quite common and this can give us more flexibility when organizing our library.

## Issue
Due to the endpoint change, you can't get DMs directly now. That makes the `bot.get_channel` function not work as intended, because as of now it's still trying to get the DM channels via the endpoint.

## Possible solutions

1. Make it so that it only returns TextChannels, and add a new `bot.get_dm_channel` to get DM channels
2. Make it so that it only returns TextChannels, and don't add a new `bot.get_dm_channel` to get DM channels
3. Make it so that it returns both TextChannels and DMChannels again

## Current workaround

```py
async def _get_channel(self, channel_id: int):

    channel = self.bot.get_channel(channel_id)
    if channel:
        return channel

    # No channel found, then we need to get the user, since channel_id is the ID of a user
    user = await self.bot.get_user_info(channel_id)

    # If the bot already has a dm_channel open with the user, then there's no need to create a new one
    if user.dm_channel:
        return user

    # Creating a new DM channel
    return await self.bot.create_dm(user)
```

I'm using the release version of Pillow, the active development branch of PIL, 
with images2gif.py.

There are three issues here.

First, please see 
https://code.google.com/p/visvis/source/browse/vvmovie/images2gif.py#413

> palette = getheader(im)[1]

In personal communication with wiredfool from the PIL, we looked through the 
PIL code and established that the palette is in fact in getheader(im)[0][-1]


Second, the fallback palette is, from 
https://code.google.com/p/visvis/source/browse/vvmovie/images2gif.py#415

> palette = PIL.ImagePalette.ImageColor

But this is a module, and not appropriate for a color palette!

The correct solution would be to use PIL.Image.convert(mode='P') to convert the 
image to palette mode.  My client code converts everything to mode='P' already 
so I didn't test this.


Finally, there are .encode('utf-8') calls scattered around the code.  These 
won't work - the data given to you by PIL is already in bytes format.  Just 
deleting them seems to be effective.

With those changes, I was able to get this routine to write nice-looking 
animated GIFs!

issue_title: Error parsing TRNAMT (should also accept commas)
issue_body:
Hello,

I have an OFX file generated by a Brazilian bank (Santander) - here we use "," as the decimal separator and the OFX have mixed "," and "." as decimal separators on the `TRNAMT` values (probably this difference occurs based on which integration they have, like the debit card or ATM systems).

Here is a sample:

```
<STMTTRN>
    <TRNTYPE>OTHER
    <DTPOSTED>20150706000000[-3:GMT]
    <TRNAMT>            -12.80
    <FITID>00451065
    <CHECKNUM>00451065
    <PAYEEID>0
    <MEMO>DEBITO VISA ELECTRON BRASIL        05/07 CAFE ALBRECHT L
</STMTTRN>
<STMTTRN>
    <TRNTYPE>OTHER
    <DTPOSTED>20150706000000[-3:GMT]
    <TRNAMT>           -200,00
    <FITID>00239183
    <CHECKNUM>00239183
    <PAYEEID>0
    <MEMO>SAQUE NO BANCO 24 HORAS
</STMTTRN>
```

I thought it was an error on the file (so Santander should fix it), but looking into the OFX specification I found that it's actually supported:

```
3.2.5 Transaction Amount <TRNAMT>
Format: Amount
Open Financial Exchange uses <TRNAMT> in any request or response that reports the total amount of an individual transaction.
Usage: Bank statement download, investment statement download, payments
```

And:

```
Amount: Amounts that do not represent whole numbers (for example, 540.32), must include a decimal point or comma to indicate the start of the fractional amount. Amounts should not include any punctuation separating thousands, millions, and so forth. The maximum value accepted depends on the client.
```

So, the "amount" types should not be represented by `ofxtools.types.Decimal` because it does not support commas as decimal separators. We should create another type to handle this properly or modify the `ofxtools.types.Decimal` to accept commas.

> Note: I had [the same error on the ofxparse library](https://github.com/jseutter/ofxparse/issues/83).

Right now I have a couple of zsh aliases to speed up using git on my dotdrop repository:
```
alias dotdrop='~/git/dotfiles/dotdrop.sh'
alias dotgit='git -C ~/git/dotfiles'
alias dotsync='dotgit pull; dotgit add -A; dotgit commit; dotgit push; dotdrop install'
```
Might not be the best naming scheme but the idea is that when I edit a dotfile on any of my pc's, I can run dotsync on the originating pc and write a commit message. Then I can just run dotsync again everywhere else and it automagically syncs and installs everything with a grand total of two commands.

I think end users would really benefit from git integration like this. I have no idea how I would start implementing something like this but I'm just putting this on the table for the contributors to consider.

This would allow the [debops.pki](https://github.com/debops/ansible-pki) Ansible role to verify signatures when deploying.

Ref: https://github.com/micahflee/onionshare/issues/221#issuecomment-220317661
Follow up for: #138

issue_title: Cannot connect to IPv6 with non-zero scope-id
issue_body: Following on debug logs posted to #868 , it looks like we are not handling ipv6 sockaddr tuples correctly. At https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/blob/fb09c0aa727141eaf0ec94c76a86b2f3b9328de8/kafka/conn.py#L260 we extract the host / port from the sockaddr. This is fine for ipv4 where sockaddr is just a 2-tuple . But in IPv6 sockaddr is a 4-tuple, including flow-info and scope-id. If those are non-zero, we will not be able to connect correctly when we call connect_ex((host, port)).

Hi all,

I'm trying to understand how relocation works in ELF file. When I test the `elf.RelocationHandler.apply_section_relocations` for section `.rela.dyn` of `sample_exe64.elf` program (in `examples` folder), it gave me `elftools.common.exceptions.ELFRelocationError: Unsupported relocation type: 6`. 

Tracing down the bug, I've found that the recipe for that `.rela.dyn` was `R_X86_64_GLOB_DAT`. Is it intentional that this recipe, as well as many other ones, are not supported?

in dwarf/structs.py, DW_FORM_ref_sig8 is defined as

`DW_FORM_ref_sig8=self.Dwarf_offset(''),`

but in my opinion, it should be

issue_title: Maya gains an extra second when parsing dates after the year 2243
issue_body:
Consider the following program:

```python
import datetime
import maya

d = datetime.datetime(2243, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1)
print(d)
print(str(d))

parsed_d = maya.parse(str(d)).datetime()
print(parsed_d)
print(str(parsed_d))

assert d == parsed_d
```

```
2243-01-01 00:00:00.000001
2243-01-01 00:00:00.000001
2243-01-01 00:00:00.000002+00:00
2243-01-01 00:00:00.000002+00:00
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "counterexample.py", line 15, in <module>
    assert d == parsed_d
AssertionError
```

This is a bug introduced somewhere in Maya – I see that Maya is using dateutil for the main machinery of its `parse` function, but if I try this with dateutil everything is fine:

```python
import datetime
from dateutil import parser

d = datetime.datetime(2243, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1)
print(d)
print(str(d))

parsed_d = parser.parse(str(d))
print(parsed_d)
print(str(parsed_d))

assert d == parsed_d
```

```
2243-01-01 00:00:00.000001
2243-01-01 00:00:00.000001
2243-01-01 00:00:00.000001
2243-01-01 00:00:00.000001
```

Environment details:

```console
$ pip freeze
dateparser==0.5.0
humanize==0.5.1
iso8601==0.1.11
jdatetime==1.8.1
-e git+git@github.com:kennethreitz/maya.git@6a4ddff215edbdce4a0f5b8bd5ba87710697b7f9#egg=maya-master

In https://github.com/nabla-c0d3/sslyze/blob/master/plugins/PluginSessionRenegotiation.py, there is a potential false positive when testing Jetty Applications. When Jetty is set to reject renegotiation, it actually completes one handshake, and then closes the socket. So an error is not thrown.

You would however, get 'An established connection was aborted by the software in your host machine' when you try to renegotiate once again since the connection is closed after performing 1 handshake.

In TLSv1.2 only supported servers, where client initiated renegotiations are possible, a message is shown - Vulnerable - Server honors client-initiated renegotiations. is this a bug in SSLyze code?

issue_title: Flush doesn't work in multiprocessing environment
issue_body:
I have been trying to implement this log handler for use by Celery workers. Celery creates process forks, but has the appropriate signals when workers start and stop that new handler instances can be started and or stopped to create the necessary threads (and stream names).

Because the child threads are ```daemon=True``` they are terminated immediately. Since the queue.get is blocking, the end token will never be read.

My thought on a solution is to alter the flush method to empty the queues in the main thread. Alternatively the child threads could be non-daemon, but that would cause a block on exiting if the user did not call flush.

Better to explain with code (I tried to extract a minimal example from our codebase):

```python
from django.db import models
from django.db.models import Q, Count, Case, When, IntegerField

class User(models.Model):
    id = models.BigAutoField(primary_key=True)
    email = models.CharField()

query = User.objects.annotate(
    gmail=Count(
      Case(When(Q(email__icontains='gmail.com'), then=1), output_field=IntegerField())) \
).filter(gmail__gte=1).order_by('gmail')

query.all()

query.all()[1:]

issue_title: Auxiliary Module with Embedded Type Executables
issue_body:
When a custom type includes embedded `module procedures`, we have previously tested them by making the subroutine/function that the embedded method points to `public`. Then the unit test driver can just call the subroutine easily using the existing framework. This worked for lots of test cases, but one broke this morning. The `gfortran` compiler failed with `Abort trap 6` error. After some digging we found out that:

- if we compiled `fpy_auxiliary` in its own folder and then copied the `.mod` and `.o` files to the unit test staging folder, the compiler would fail.
- if we copied `fpy_auxiliary.f90` to the staging folder and compiled the unit test completely by itself, with all the source code, then the compiler would *not* fail.

Evidently, the two versions of the `fpy_auxiliary` files were different. The only difference in code between the two was the `public` statement in the unit test version of the module that made the subroutine being tested public. We adjusted the call to the subroutine manually by calling `type%subroutine` and omitting the first argument, and then recompiled using the `.mod` and `.o` versions and everything, but then we ran into an error where the compiler also wanted the auxiliary module's dependencies... So we compiled using the auxiliary shared library and then it worked.

So, to fix this problem, here are the steps:
1. Compile the auxiliary shared library just the way we have been doing it.
2. Instead of copying the `fpy_auxiliary.o` and `.mod` files, symlink to the shared library.
3. Prevent embedded methods from being made public  in `fortpy.testing.method._check_exec_public`.
4. Change the call signature for embedded methods to use the `%`-syntax and omit the first argument.
5. Execute the unit test drivers with the prefix `DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH=...` so that the shared library can be located by the executable.

issue_title: Wrong module guess for click event with multiple volume blocks
issue_body:
I have defined two volume blocks for two different pulse devices. When defining click events, clicking on either block runs the onClick action of the first module.

The configuration looks like this:
```
volume master {
    device = "pulse:0"
    on_click 1 = "exec pavucontrol"
nus"
}

volume slave {
    device = "pulse:1"
}
```

Log when running py3status with this configuration and clicking on the first block:
```
Jan 29 18:13:13 genius /py3status[24905]: received event {'y': 7, 'instance': 'pulse:0.Master.0', 'x': 1061, 'name': 'volume', 'button': 1}
Jan 29 18:13:13 genius /py3status[24905]: trying to dispatch event to module "volume master"
```

Clicking on the second block does not dispatch the event to the second module ```volume slave```, but again to the first one ```volume master```:
```
Jan 29 18:13:37 genius /py3status[24905]: received event {'y': 12, 'instance': 'pulse:1.Master.0', 'x': 1108, 'name': 'volume', 'button': 1}
Jan 29 18:13:37 genius /py3status[24905]: trying to dispatch event to module "volume master"
```

The instance in the reply does not correspond to the descriptor ```master/slave```, but is a concatenation of the ```device```, ```mixer``` and ```mixer_id```. [The guessing method for the module](https://github.com/ultrabug/py3status/blob/master/py3status/__init__.py#L806) takes this into account but relies on the presence of all three configuration parameters. If any of the three parameter is not set, the guessing will not work correctly. Instead, the check should just ignore unset parameters and get the closest match for device, mixer, and then mixer_id.

As a **workaround**, define all three configuration parameters explicitly:
```
volume slave {
    device = "pulse:1"
    mixer = "master"
    mixer_id = "0"
}
```

issue_title: Protocol relative url doesn't need to be fixed
issue_body:
It is safe to use the `fix_protocol` parameter even when the url has proper protocol:
`>>> get_tld("http://www.google.co.uk")`
`'google.co.uk'`
`>>> get_tld("http://www.google.co.uk", fix_protocol=True)`
`'google.co.uk'`

But not in case of protocol relative url though it is valid for `urlparse`
`>>> get_tld("//www.google.co.uk")`
`'google.co.uk'`
`>>> get_tld("//www.google.co.uk", fix_protocol=True)`

This is a suggestion to make a small modification to the `Model` class definitions, which will improve speed and reduce memory usage.

Currently each class based on `TwitterModel` constructs during its `__init__` a dictionary `param_defaults`, which is bound to the instance and which never changes.

The suggestion is to move these out of the `__init__` function (but still within the class definition) and remove the " `self.` " so that the value becomes a class data variable, and is constructed only once and bound to the class.

Example below for `Media`. No other changes are needed since `self.param_defaults` within a method will find the value in the class. The benefit is that instances will consume less memory and be faster to construct. (The only issue is that any modifications to param_defaults would affect all instances, but it seems clear this is intended to be a read-only element of the data structure).

```
class Media(TwitterModel):

    """A class representing the Media component of a tweet. """
    param_defaults = {
            'display_url': None,
            'expanded_url': None,
            'ext_alt_text': None,
            'id': None,
            'media_url': None,
            'media_url_https': None,
            'sizes': None,
            'type': None,
            'url': None,
            'video_info': None,
        }

    def __init__(self, **kwargs):

        for (param, default) in self.param_defaults.items():
            setattr(self, param, kwargs.get(param, default))

```

issue_title: AttributeError when test client is named self.app
issue_body:
When the test client is named `self.app`, i.e. you have a situation like
```
    def setUp(self):
        self.app = application.test_client()
```

Then when `assertRedirects` tries to access the Flask instance at `self.app` it instead gets the `FlaskClient` instance and crashes as below.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.9/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/unittest/case.py", line 329, in run
    testMethod()
  File "/Users/bistenes/Code/socos/jive/server/src/tests/test_pages.py", line 118, in test_logs_in_user
    self.assertRedirects(rv, '/manage')
  File "/Users/bistenes/Code/socos/jive/server/src/lib/flask_testing/utils.py", line 278, in assertRedirects
    server_name = self.app.config.get('SERVER_NAME') or 'localhost'
AttributeError: 'FlaskClient' object has no attribute 'config'
```

I'm pretty surprised this wasn't a problem before 0.6.0 -- you would think that resolving `self.app` elsewhere, e.g. for `self.app.get(...)` would also be a problem, but it apparently wasn't. The obvious solution is to not name the test client `self.app`, but rather, say, `self.client`.

This is mostly a request for documentation -- somewhere it should advise against doing this. It's also, of course, a note to those running into this confusing error in the future. Actual code change is probably not called for here.

Hi @sepandhaghighi I'm revieing `pycm` for JOSS.
First, you all have done a great job and the library is well put-together. I'm sorry I haven't started the review sooner. I think the library as-is is very close to "accept". There are just a couple of minor revisions I think might be helpful.

Of those minor revisions, the one I would most like to see is a thorough documentation of the API.

At the bare minimum, the user should be able to type `help(ConfusionMatrix)` (or `ConfusionMatrix?` in Ipython) and get some sort of useful help. As you probably know, the way to fix this would be to add a docstring to the `ConfusionMatrix` class. This docstring should state what the acceptable types for `y_true` and `y_pred` are--are only `lists` of `ints` and `np.ndarrays` of `ints` valid? Including more brief versions of the examples from the README.md (without the entire output of the `print(cm)` statement) would probably also be helpful.

I think it would also be helpful to state what data types are acceptable in plain English on your README.md. A potential user will want to know if `pymc` can accept their data in its present state or if they're going to have to convert it somehow to the right format.

I will need to finish this review tomorrow but I wanted to get it started for you as soon as possible. I hope my review will be helpful and we can quickly get your library into JOSS so more people can appreciate it.

I intend to implement these suggestions in the future, but I'm opening this "issue" to serve as a reminder and also to gather opinions about them.
##### First suggestion
In commit ee3f12a3c769185d6097f4c853ac7a1ddc1d0bea, I've set the Bandcamp scraper to **never redownload** songs, even if the "-f" flag is not specified. Simply put, I think this should be the default behaviour and should be replicated to all other scrapers. The specific code can currently be found between lines 294 and 311: https://github.com/Miserlou/SoundScrape/blob/master/soundscrape/soundscrape.py#L294-L311

As I've also noted in commit ee3f12a3c769185d6097f4c853ac7a1ddc1d0bea, implementing the first suggestion could raise the idea of adding a "-r" flag, so the user may actually allow redownloads.

issue_body:
Looks good to me. A few details:

- [x] _Version: Does the release version given match the GitHub release (v0.0.14)?_
No: I see nashpy-0.0.17

- [x] _References: Do all archival references that should have a DOI list one (e.g., papers, datasets, software)?_
Only one reference in paper.bib has a DOI.

- [x] Suggestions: I wonder if you could give more real-world usage examples and algorithms limitations. In particular, I tried with a few large random matrices 0 sum games and the game.support_enumeration() computation takes forever.

PS: testing at https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1UGrlXawmReLX4-y_vQqQeXhFEttfOWW9

(See https://github.com/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/904)

I do not want to sound pedantic — a lot of work has clearly gone into making this work with different browsers and I very much appreciate what has been done. 

The thing is, the code is not very readable. All the multi-line vars, `||` concatenations, and shorthand variable names make the code very hard to interpret (and with very little gain, given that you ship a minified version). The code should be refactored.

Just wondering if there are any caveats/instructions to deployment? (eg. in my case, I'm hoping to set up a quick development server with Heroku and wondering if I can use this wrapper with waitress). I'm assuming no, since aiohttp has it's own http server...but I know there is support for aiohttp with Gunicorn...

Let me know if this makes sense. I'm sure I may not be making the most sense as I'm just getting started with asyncio...and trying to make it work with Django...

issue_title: Issue with column index level for grouping in Excel
issue_labels: [{'id': 753729862, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/jmcnamara/XlsxWriter/labels/awaiting%20user%20feedback', 'name': 'awaiting user feedback', 'color': 'e892cf', 'default': False}, {'id': 23779652, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/jmcnamara/XlsxWriter/labels/question', 'name': 'question', 'color': 'cc317c', 'default': True}, {'id': 115685664, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/jmcnamara/XlsxWriter/labels/ready%20to%20close', 'name': 'ready to close', 'color': 'bfe5bf', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Hi,

I am using XlsxWriter to produce nested column groups, where the first column is in both levels 1 and 2. This works fine when constructing in Excel but xlsxwriter appears to allow only one level assignment; setting a column to level 2 as part of a subgroup gets overwritten when assigning that column as one of the limits to a larger group in level 1, and the entire subgroup loses its grouping.

There *are* little dots still above the phantom-subgroups columns, suggesting Excel is aware that there is something different about the columns, but there is no collapse-able bracket above the columns.

I am using Python version 3.5.2 and XlsxWriter 1.0.2 and Excel from Office 365 ProPlus Version 1708 (Build 8431.2242).

Here is some code that demonstrates the problem:

```python
import xlsxwriter

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('hello.xlsx')
worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

worksheet.set_column(3, 5, 14, None, {'level': 2})
worksheet.set_column(7, 9, 14, None, {'level': 2})
worksheet.set_column(3, 10, 14, None, {'level': 1})


workbook.close()
```

issue_title: Checking bozo will cause parser to fail (not checking will parse fine)
issue_body:

    import feedparser
    import json

    info = {}

    #if parser.bozo == 1:
    #    info['bozo_message'] = parser.bozo_exception

With the bozo check commented out this runs fine and prints the json string with the title, uncomment the bozo check and you get:

I would expect this to work the same regardless of checking the bozo exception.

issue_title: Nested serializer fields aren't described in the request body
issue_body:
* DRF OpenAPI version: 1.3.0
* Python version: 3.6.2
* Operating System: Ubuntu 16.04

### Description

If main serializer has any nested serializers except ListSerializer (it's handled correctly), fields of this sub-serializers doesn't shown on html page with api description. 

### What I Did

```
class PersonSerializer(serializers.Serializer):
    first_name = serializers.CharField()
    last_name = serializers.CharField()

class DocumentSerializer(serializers.Serializer):
    doc_type= serializers.IntegerField()
    number = serializers.CharField()

class CheckDocRequestSerializer(serializers.Serializer):
    person = PersonSerializer()
    documents = serializers.ListField(child=DocumentSerializer())
```

Main serializer is `CheckDocRequestSerializer` and  in this case fields `doc_type`, `number` will be shown for `DocumentSerializer` but `PersonSerializer` will be described as `object` without any sub-elements.

"Request example":
```
{
  "person": {},
  "documents": [
    {
      "doc_type": 0,
      "number": "string"
    }
  ]
}
```

Broadly, I will try to reduce the number of factories being used and have less dependency injection. The dependency injection was mostly designed to make the code more testable but I have never written tests to take advantage of that so it is just a cost to readability at the moment.

Example:
```
plexapi.video.Movie.edit(**kwargs)
```

```
class Movie(Video, Playable):

    def edit(self, **kwargs):
        """ Edit a movie.
            
            Parameters:
                kwargs (dict): Dict of settings to edit.
        """
        if 'id' not in kwargs:
            kwargs['id'] = self.ratingKey
        if 'type' not in kwargs:
            kwargs['type'] = utils.searchType(self.type)

        part = '/library/sections/%s/all?%s' % (self.key, urlencode(kwargs))
        self._server.query(part, method=self._server._session.put)
```

* [Removes ALL collections from ALL movies](https://gist.github.com/JonnyWong16/34878448ab45dfffffa930f5cf8c252a)

Ideally something like `plexapi.video.Movie.addCollection()`, `.addLabel()`, `.addGenre()`, etc. would be useful. (Reference #37)

```
class Movie(Video, Playable):

    def addCollection(self, collection=None, locked=True):
        """ Add the movie to a collection.
            
            Parameters:
                collection (str): Name of collection to add.
                locked (bool): True to lock the collection.
        """
        if collection is not None:
            collection_key = "collection[{key}].tag.tag".format(key=len(self.collections))
            data = {"collection.locked": utils.cast(int, locked),
                    collection_key: collection
                    }

            return self.edit(**data)
```

I didn't see anyone having raised this issue before, so I thought perhaps you might not realize: the word "voluptuous" in english has a heavily sexual connotation.  [The very first noun associated with it in the dictionary definition](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/voluptuous) is "nudes".  

[Using unnecessarily sexualized language can create an environment that is unduly hostile to women](http://geekfeminism.wikia.com/wiki/Sexualized_environment), even if this is not intended. Many of the biases against women that such language provokes are totally unconscious, so even if the intent is not harmful, it can be bad regardless.

Finally, the _very first_ thing in the README says "despite the name" so it seems that perhaps it's occurred to you that this isn't a great name for a library.

In summary: in the interests of creating a more inclusive community, please come up with a new name for this library.  Thank you for reading!

I would like to define a schema for a tuple: 

```
Schema( ( Any( int, 'foo', 'bar') ) )( ('foo', 'bar) )
```

I found that voluptuous does not understand the tuple information. For this I had to trick it using: 

```
Schema( All(tuple, Coerce(list), [ Any(int, 'foo', 'bar') ]))
```

But this time I get this error:

```
/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/voluptuous/schema_builder.pyc in validate_mapping(path, iterable, out)
    270                         # include if it's not marked for removal
    271                         if not is_remove:
--> 272                             out[new_key] = cval
    273                         else:
    274                             remove_key = True

TypeError: unhashable type: 'list'
```

So I eventually wrote this workaround: 

```
Schema( All(tuple, Coerce(list), [ Any(int, 'foo', 'bar') ], Coerce(tuple)))
```

issue_title: Why catch and swallow KeyboardInterrupt exceptions?
issue_body:
Hi,

First, thanks for your work on inquirer, I've used it to replace some custom interactive mode prompts on a cli app and it's a huge improvement on what we had before :-)

On thing that I found odd though, it the way inquirer catches KeyboardInterrupt exceptions, and doesn't re-raise them. This surprised me because I'd expect these exceptions to bubble up to the main/calling application, where they can be handled consistently regardless of where they occur.

Is there a specific reason for catching these exceptions within inquirer itself, or would you be open to accepting a pull request that either removes the code to catch them or at least re-raises them? Personally, I'd vote for the former as I don't think it's the responsibility of a library/package like inquirer to handle these exceptions at all, but then, I'm not really a python programmer, so maybe I'm missing something here.

Thanks

Mark

Version 0.11.0 contains a major change in the way plugin classes are declared:

Instead of adding a list of `PartialFormField`s named `glossary_fields`, we now can add these
fields to the plugin class, as we would in a Django `forms.Form` or `models.Model`, for
instance

``` python
class MyPlugin(CascadePluginBase):
    name = "My Plugin"
    ... other attributes ...
    some_attr = GlossaryField(a_widget, label="A Label", initial=some_value)
```

instead of

``` python
class MyPlugin(CascadePluginBase):
    name = "My Plugin"
    ... other attributes ...
    glossary_fields = (
        PartialFormField('some_attr', a_widget, label="A Label", initial=some_value),
    )
```

What's your opinion about it?

BTW: this feature is available on branch `glossary_fields`

issue_body:
Hi,

I am using angular v1.5.9 and smart-table v2.1.0.  I have searched for answers to my question below, but have not been able to find any that address the specific promise/async attributes of it - apologies if this has been addressed somewhere and/or is a duplicate - if so, kindly please refer me there.

I have been using both st-table="displayedItems" and st-safe-src="items" directives in combination with st-sort.  This combination successfully and reliably works for nearly all conditions I have run across.  This includes scenarios where the st-sort is function based.  However, I recently encountered an need to have a function based st-sort that returns a promise instead of the value directly (i.e. the function will asynchronously resolve the value, at a slightly later time - when it becomes available).

My question is, should the st-sort be expected to produce a predictable ordering of the rows in that case?  If so, it does not appear to be.  I am theorizing that this may be do to the fact that the associated values are not available to the sort algorithm all up front - but rather straggle in in an unpredictable order and time frame.  By the way, no errors or other indications are returned that a problem may exist.  Should I expect this to work, or is this a known limitation?  Are there additional items that can be done to make it work on my part or otherwise - if so, I'd greatly appreciate any tips or thoughts you may have?

issue_title: Review the message for skipping dependent tests
issue_body:
PR #11 changed the message provided when a test is skipped due to missing dependencies in that the name of the skipped test was added.  There are however still inconsistencies and issues with these messages that the PR did not address.

In the most basic case, the skip messages look like:
```
$ py.test -rs basic.py
============================= test session starts ==============================
[...]
=========================== short test summary info ============================
SKIP [1] /usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pytest_dependency.py:65: test_e depends on test_c
SKIP [1] /usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pytest_dependency.py:65: test_c depends on test_a

================ 2 passed, 2 skipped, 1 xfailed in 0.02 seconds ================
```
The most obvious issue is the trace line `/usr/.../pytest_dependency.py:65` that appears in each skip message.  It is due to the fact that skipping a test in pytest is an exception and this is the trace with the line of code that calls `pytest.skip()`.  It is always the same in each skip message and clutters the message without containing any useful bit of information.  It should be removed.  Unfortunately, adding this trace is hard coded deep in the internals of pytest, so it won't be easy to get rid of it.

The name of the dependent test appearing in the message is actually the name of the function or method of the test:
```
$ py.test -rs named.py 
============================= test session starts ==============================
[...]
=========================== short test summary info ============================
SKIP [1] /usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pytest_dependency.py:65: test_c depends on a
SKIP [1] /usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pytest_dependency.py:65: test_e depends on c

================ 2 passed, 2 skipped, 1 xfailed in 0.01 seconds ================
```
For the name of the dependent, the message ignores the name as set with the `name` argument to the `pytest.mark.dependency()` marker, while for the dependency, this setting is taken into account.  This is somewhat inconsistent.

This inconsistency would be acceptable, if it would lead to unambiguous names.  Unfortunately, this is not the case.  Consider the following example:
```python
import pytest

def test_b():
    pass

class TestClass(object):

    @pytest.mark.dependency()
    @pytest.mark.xfail(reason="deliberate fail")
    def test_a(self):
        assert False

    @pytest.mark.dependency(depends=["TestClass::test_a"])
    def test_b(self):
        pass
```
The output looks like:
```
$ py.test -rs testclass.py 
============================= test session starts ==============================
[...]
=========================== short test summary info ============================
SKIP [1] /usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pytest_dependency.py:65: test_b depends on TestClass::test_a

================ 1 passed, 1 skipped, 1 xfailed in 0.01 seconds ================
```
This is misleading, one may believe that the test function `test_b()` had been skipped, but it was the method `TestClass::test_b()` in fact.

Would you be interested in providing binary wheels for windows, mac, and `manylinux`?

issue_title: Files are always re-uploaded to S3 storage
Collectstatic creates multiple files, some empty
issue_body:
Not sure if this is a django-storages issue, but maybe someone has an idea. 
I am upgrading an app...

Current versions:
```
django 1.8.17 
django-storages 1.5.1
```

Goal:
```
django 1.11.12
django-storages 1.6.6
```

Storage:
```
class StaticS3BotoStorage(ManifestFilesMixin, S3BotoStorage):
    location = 'static'
    file_overwrite = True
    preload_metadata = True
```
or
```
class StaticS3BotoStorage(CachedFilesMixin, S3BotoStorage):
    location = 'static'
    file_overwrite = True
    preload_metadata = True
```


With the initial setup, collectstatic worked fine, including collectstatic --clear.

After the upgrade, collectstatic --clear fails (no files are deleted).
collecstatic does copy files, however, sometimes it creates two versions of the same file. In this particular example, I get base.hash1.css and base.hash2.css. base.hash2.css is empty, so pages open, but do not render correctly.

If I don't use CachedFilesMixin or ManifestFilesMixin, collectstatic works fine, but clear still fails.

I tested different combinations of django 1.11 and all version of django-storages 1.6.0 to 1.6.6. They all seem to behave the same.

I find it quite odd that there is a major storage backend completely left out of the docs. 
While I was searching for a Google Cloud Storage backend for Django, the only one from a reputable repo appeared to be with the [apache-libcloud](http://djangostorages.readthedocs.org/en/latest/backends/apache_libcloud.html) backend. It took a much deeper investigation to realize that there actually is a GCS backend implemented in django-storages, it's just not mentioned.

There needs to be a doc section on `storages.backends.gs`. I would be willing to make it - it seems that you could copy a lot from the S3Boto docs. One quirk is that to use you must get a interoperability key from the Google Cloud Console.

http://readthedocs.org/projects/django-storages/builds/ shows that the last build was 9.5 months ago.

This means recent changes are not being displayed in https://django-storages.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ and there is no documentation compiled for the most recent version (1.6.6: https://django-storages.readthedocs.io/en/1.6.6/).

I suggest change **readme.rst** and add a remark when is a good practice add **newline** param to **open** method. 

See foot notes in documentation: https://docs.python.org/3/library/csv.html

If you do not put this parameter then the parameter **lineterminator** gives problems. In my case adds an extra blank line to record a csv.

```
from backports import csv
import io

def read_csv(filename):
    with io.open(filename, newline='', encoding='utf-8') as f:
        for row in csv.reader(f):
            yield row

def write_csv(filename, rows):
    with io.open(filename, 'w', newline='', encoding='utf-8') as f:
        writer = csv.writer(f)
        for row in rows:
            writer.writerows(row)
```

issue_title: Unavailable DB causes generic 500 if `ATOMIC_REQUESTS` is enabled
issue_body:
When [`ATOMIC_REQUESTS`](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/ref/settings/#std:setting-DATABASE-ATOMIC_REQUESTS) is enabled any database error (e.g. server unreachable) causes the generic django 500 error page to be shown. 

This can be prevent by decorating the status view with [`@transaction.non_atomic_requests`](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/topics/db/transactions/#django.db.transaction.non_atomic_requests)

We use testing.postgresql in our automated tests that fire-up a local instance of PostgreSQL using `initdb` and then run our schema creation and data seeding tasks.

For the past few months we've been getting test failures due with this error:

> initdb failed: 'FATAL:  could not create semaphores: No space left on device
DETAIL:  Failed system call was semget(1, 17, 03600).
HINT:  This error does *not* mean that you have run out of disk space.  It occurs when either the system limit for the maximum number of semaphore sets (SEMMNI), or the system wide maximum number of semaphores (SEMMNS), would be exceeded.  You need to raise the respective kernel parameter.  Alternatively, reduce PostgreSQL's consumption of semaphores by reducing its max_connections parameter.
The PostgreSQL documentation contains more information about configuring your system for PostgreSQL.
child process exited with exit code 1
initdb: removing contents of data directory "/tmp/tmpctGr9I/data"

These tests run on an Amazon EC2 instance running Amazon Linux 2015.09 x64. Here's the output from `ipcs -ls` showing semaphore configuration:

> [ec2-user@ip-127-0-0-1 etc]$ ipcs -ls
------ Semaphore Limits --------
max number of arrays = 128
max semaphores per array = 250
max semaphores system wide = 32000
max ops per semop call = 32
semaphore max value = 32767
[ec2-user@ip-127-0-0-1 etc]$

We see the same issue on both Mac OS X with PostgreSQL 9.4 as well as on our Amazon EC2 instances running PostgreSQL 9.3. PostgreSQL is installed via `yum` on the EC2 instances via our build script, and `brew` on Mac.

We're unsure if it's a bug in PostgreSQL or testing.postgresql, but we thought we'd start here.

We're currently using testing.postgresql 1.1.2 (we're in the process of upgrading to the latest version - it is currently incompatible with our builds).

On Ubuntu 14.04 with PostgreSQL 9.3 database initialization with initdb is the slowest part of testing.postgresql: it takes around 2.5 seconds to create data directory with default contents.

While it's possible to create PG data directory outside of testing.postgresql, cache it, copy for each test and pass to `testing.postgresql.Postgresql` with `copy_data_from` argument, it's a lot of work and requires to reimplementing of some of the testing.postgresql functionality (e.g. search of `initdb` utility).

I propose to implement caching of `initdb` result inside PostgreSQL and use it by default.

This can be done pretty straightforward and I can prepare PR for this issue if you think my approach is satisfactory:

1. Add dependency for library that provides API to work with user cache directory (e.g. [appdirs](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/appdirs)). Cache directory will be used for caching initdb result. Cache should be verioned by initdb version (`initdb -V`) and by testing.postgresql version.

2. Add option to `testing.postgresql.Postgresql` to disable cache. e.g. `cache=False`.
   In `testing.postgresql.Postgresql` if cache is enabled: check if cache for the current version of initdb+testing.postgresql exists, if no, create it and fill it with `initdb`; copy cached directory content to a temporary PG data directory.

I know it is already mentioned as dependency in [wvfbwrapper repo](https://github.com/cgoldberg/xvfbwrapper#system-requirements) but I think it´s worth to mention it here, too, to keep users from running into an error like shown below and to make documentation even more user friendly as it already is ;-)

```
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/xvfbwrapper.py", line 27, in __init__
    raise EnvironmentError(msg)
OSError: Can not find Xvfb. Please install it and try again.
```

I would like to consult improvement of "Active Directory support" section in README. I'm only very occasional Active Directory user therefore the following proposals needs to be reviewed by someone else with stronger AD knowledge.

### `sAMAccountName` is AD's equivalent to `uid` attribute
Thus for `AD LDAP_AUTH_USER_FIELDS` needs to be

    LDAP_AUTH_USER_FIELDS = {
        'username': 'sAMAccountName',
        'first_name': 'givenName',
        'last_name': 'sn',
        'email': 'mail',
    }

See https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms679635(v=vs.85).aspx.

### AD's object class of users is `user` instead of `inetOrgPerson`
Despite `inetOrgPerson` class exists in AD schema for compatibility reasons, regular users most usually have 'user' class instead.

    LDAP_AUTH_OBJECT_CLASS = 'user'

See https://social.technet.microsoft.com/wiki/contents/articles/20289.inetorgperson-class-in-active-directory.aspx.
Drop support for Python 2.6. Add 3.5 and 3.6.

issue_title: Blog post slug with the same name as template file renders the template file instead
issue_body:
# Actual behavior
When I was testing my zinnia install, I created a post called 'test'. When I wanted to view the post, it rendered a template file called 'test.html' instead, which was located in my templates folder but displayed the expected URL. I tried again, using a slug name called 'base' and it rendered base.html.

I also took the liberty of trying this on the zinnia live demo site with the slug 'base' and it again redirected me to 'base.html' from the zinnia project but showed the url as 'http://demo.django-blog-zinnia.com/blog/2016/12/05/base/' but there was no error message.

# Expected behavior
Even if the filename matches the slug, I was under the impression that the blog-post is identified with a combination of information (i.e. year, month, date) more than just the slug and so would always show the post even if there was a potential conflict.

# Steps to reproduce the issue

  1. Create blog post with a slug with the same name as a html file in your django project, e.g. 'test' --> 'test.html' in templates, 'base' --> 'base.html' in templates
  2. Open blog post called 'test', 'base' etc. and see the attempt to render 'test.hml' or 'base.html' in the blog post. 

# Specifications

 - Zinnia version: 0.17
 - Django version: 1.9
 - Python version: 3.5
 - Operating system: Mac OS X

issue_title: Stream parser throws away data if too much read from socket
issue_body:
If the socket reads more data than requested (as in the SSL case), then stream parser can get more data than it requested. 

This means that the `some_bytes` variable [here](https://github.com/Lawouach/WebSocket-for-Python/blob/b2010a5c2af8464894e4ed6d82a7285580815acb/ws4py/streaming.py#L184) can contain more than one frame's worth of data.

The frame parser then correctly reads one frame out of the data, but the rest of the data is thrown away.

issue_body:
hi: 
     I use flask socketio with gevent_uwsgi mode，and use nginx as proxy .

uwsgi  key option:
http = :5000
http-websockets = 1
processes = 1
gevent = 10000
lazy-apps = true

many uwsgi instance run at the same time, and every instance use one http port like 5000, 5001 and so on

meanwhile in front of uwsgi instance,  I use many nginx proxy,  every nginx forward socktio request by nginx upstream ip_hash.  although socketio requests with same sid  may be hashed to different nginx, but every nginx use the same configuration to hash the request to the same uwsgi worker.  In my scene, client use various browser,  so socketio use websocket or long polling

sometimes I find the uwsgi worker cpu is so high,  nearly 100%. and I strace the uwsgi worker, find it busy with select system call 
select(42, [41], [], [], {0, 0})        = 1 (in [41], left {0, 0})

uwsgi worker log show many 500 or 400 requests,  but It seems that no so many clients use socketio connection at the same time.  exceptionally,  even I reload uwsgi worker,  restart uwsgi master, the cpu still very high immediately after reload.  and strace show uwsgi worker still busy with select call.

I've considered several reasons:

issue_title: general support for command line options
issue_body:
It may be a good idea to generally allow to pass command line options to the compilers. (See #56 and #61).

For example:

```
STATIC_PRECOMPILER_COMPILERS = (
    ('static_precompiler.compilers.Babel', {"executable": "/usr/bin/babel", options=["--modules", "amd"]}),
)
```

However some options may causes conflicts and cause the precompiler to malfunction. I think it should be made clear in the documentation that these option should be only used at one's own risk.

The python-cloudant client (version 0.5.10) was providing async mode for database operations. However, in the 2.x.x version async mode is not supported. 

Last week, we experience long delays for put, get operations ranging from (72 seconds to 1 hour) using python client v 0.5.10 w/o asycn mode. Support team recommended us to move to version 2.x.x  To prevent, similar problems happening, we ask to have async mode. For details see Case #72985.
PS: We also look forward to contributing to this repo, once we finished moved to new client version.
Thanks

issue_title: Some wrong with mxnet on spark: params.jars = jars.split(",|:")
issue_body: The method setExecutorJars in org.apache.mxnet.spark.MXNet.scala has 'params.jars = jars.split(",|:")' sentence. If the jars from s3://path or hdfs://path, there will be wrong jar path, for example s3 or hdfs or others. Perhaps 'params.jars = jars.split(",")' is recommended.

Others might be due to incomplete information, making the issues not actionable. Some may be issues on APIs that are deprecated, and better solution/API has been developed. **What do I need to do?**
If the issue is a problem that hasn't been solved and is still impacting you, you have the choice of commenting on the closed issue and asking for reopen. issue_title: pipchecker doesn't find misconfigured pypi packages
issue_body:
I've encountered problems with running pipchecker when there's a package which is misconfigured on pypi with underscores instead of dashes.  For example:

```
% manage.py pipchecker
django-celery-beat 1.0.1         release is not on pypi (check capitalization and/or --extra-index-url)
django-polymorphic 1.3           release is not on pypi (check capitalization and/or --extra-index-url)
```

Each of these packages seems to have been set up incorrectly to use underscores instead of dashes, and I've filed bugs for both of these packages to change their pypi entries (hopefully)
https://github.com/django-polymorphic/django-polymorphic/issues/319#issuecomment-345702344
https://github.com/celery/django-celery-beat/issues/85

But I also think it would be great if pipchecker could be more resilient.  I've tested a fix that seems to work which when searching the parsed pypi xml data searches for both the proper name, and a transformed name where dashes are replaced by underscores.

`available = pypi.package_releases(req["pip_req"].name, True)`
->
`available = pypi.package_releases(req["pip_req"].name, True) or  pypi.package_releases(req["pip_req"].name.replace('-','_'), True)`

In looking at the documentation for setting `NOTEBOOK_ARGUMENTS` in a Django settings file, it seems to imply that you should enter a set of full keys and their values, so I assumed that I would need to enter a setting for `--notebook-dir` like this:

```
NOTEBOOK_ARGUMENTS = [
    '--notebook-dir=/path/to/notebooks',
]
```

but after reading https://github.com/django-extensions/django-extensions/blob/af9f78c5935b61afaaadd2a989df2bddfab58f0b/django_extensions/management/commands/shell_plus.py#L209 I realized that I'd have to enter it like this for it to work:

```
NOTEBOOK_ARGUMENTS = [
    '--notebook-dir', '/path/to/notebooks',
]
```

Should the other examples be changed to reflect this syntax?
Should probably just except Exception.

Hi there,

We encountered encoding issues when trying to send Swedish or French text. We might want to adapt [the following code](https://github.com/facebook/facebook-python-ads-sdk/blob/master/facebookads/api.py#L386-L387):
```
        if params:
            params = _top_level_param_json_encode(params)
            keyvals = ['%s=%s' % (key, urllib.parse.quote(value))
                       for key, value in params.items()]
            call['body'] = '&'.join(keyvals)
```

At first, we thought (as always with encoding) that we should do it ourselves before handing data to the Api, but the issue is that when reading data, the api will return `unicode` object instead of `basestring` object, hence it makes it difficult to handle every situation. For instance:
```
data = {...}
data = {key: value.encode("utf-8") for key, value in data.iteritems()}
creative = AdCreative().remote_create(params=data)  # this one will work
creative.remote_read(fields=["name", ...])  # let's say the name contains utf-8
adgroup_name = "Adgroup for " + creative["name"]  # here we have utf-8
adgroup_data = {"name": "adgroup_name", ...}
adgroup = AdGroup().remote_create(params=adgroup_data)  # this call will fail
```
The exception will be `KeyError: u'\xe9'` when calling the `urllib.parse.quote` method.


The fix is fairly easy and easily testable, let me know if you're interested in a PR, I can provide it quickly

issue_title: Arguments were lost in global Ajax event
issue_body:
I confirmed that 
```
$(document).ajaxSend(function(a,b,c){
   // b and c are undefined!!
});
```
In pure jQuery, **b** is jqXHR, and **c** is ajax option.

Is it correct action of this library?

I know why it occurred.
In Line 120 of event.js
```
jQuery.event.trigger( name, null, elem, true );
```
Second argument is specified **null**.
It works same as pure jQuery, if second and subsequent elements of arguments was passed to.
```
jQuery.event.trigger( name, [].slice.call(arguments,1), elem, true );
```

Upper code makes good results for me, but it may make other bugs.
So, I didn't fix it.

Is it a bug?
