issue_title: Problem appending strings to a bcolz array
issue_body:
When appending strings to a bcolz array with the following code:

    bz = bcolz.carray(np.array([], dtype="S500"))
    for i in range(1000):
        bz.append(np.array(["xxx"]))
        if len(bz) >= 2 and type(bz[1]) == np.ndarray:
            print("oops")

at some point (actually when i == bz.chunklen), the elements of the bz array have their 'bytes' type changed and become 'ndarray'.

If I specify: bz = bcolz.carray(np.array([], dtype="S500"), expectedlen=1000), things seem to go ok.

We have `bcolz.ones` and `bcolz.zeros` but no `bcolz.empty`.  If I want to create a large bcolz carray of a datetime or string dtype what is the best way?

Is there a way to do this for record dtypes and ctable as well?

Following the documentation example I want to retrieve pileup from 197396820 to 197396822 bases of chr1. 
```python
    samfile = pysam.AlignmentFile('myfile.bam', 'rb')
    for pileupcolumn in samfile.pileup("chr1", 197396820, 197396822):
        print ("\ncoverage at base %s = %s" % (pileupcolumn.pos, pileupcolumn.n))
```

Here's start, interest region (middle) and end of the output I'm getting:

issue_title: [Bug] parser: Navigation widget empty if no DIVISION is found before SECTION
issue_body:
Many programs have their headers in copybooks. A current sample I have has

```
       COPY myheader.
       77  myvar1    pic x.
       LINKAGE SECTION.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
```

The navigation widget contains "No Data". If I uncomment LINKAGE SECTION the navigation widget contains everything from the PROCEDURE DIVISION on.

Of course it would be nice if the parser assumes "missing" DIVISIONS if it finds anything that "looks" like an entry (in the given sample assuming DATA DIVSION, as the parser doesn't need to understand COBOL the actual DIVISION doesn't matter, the navigation pane would be fine if "[assumed] DIVISION" is added internally).

## System information


- OpenCobolIDE: 4.6.1
- GnuCOBOL: 1.1.0
- Python: 3.4.3 (32bits)
- Qt: 5.4.1
- PyQt: 5.4.1
- pyqode.core: 2.6.3
- pyqode.cobol: 2.6.1
- pyqode.qt: 2.6.0
- pygments: 2.0.2
- QDarkStyle: 2.0

It would be nice if test configurations were executed in the same order that they are added with add_config. I think this could be achieved by replacing self._configs = {} with self._configs = [collections.OrderedDict](https://docs.python.org/2/library/collections.html#collections.OrderedDict). The ordering of a regular [dictionary](https://docs.python.org/2/tutorial/datastructures.html#dictionaries) is undefined: "It is best to think of a dictionary as an unordered set of key: value pairs"

issue_title: Let VUnit execute simulators which are inside a container or VM, from outside
issue_body:
Because VUnit requires the simulator to be available in the path, and executable names (vsim, vcom, etc.) are 'hardcoded', VUnit must be installed in the same docker image as the simulator. In a use case, I have two images, one with GHDL and a different one with QuestaSim, and VUnit is installed in both of them. Indeed, because I have versions with and without VUnit, there are four images. Each time VUnit is updated, two of them have to be rebuilt.

My proposal is to make VUnit execute simulators which are inside a container, from outside:

- Have a local python installation, but do not install simulators in the host.
  - This would allow you not to compile GHDL in each test, but still test multiple python versions with the latest GHDL in travis.
- Build a single VUnit docker image that can interact with simulator containers.

In the latter, I would have three images instead of four, and I would have to rebuild a single one. Note how this would scale with more simulators. Furthermore, if the VUnit docker image was generated in the vunit travis-ci flow, I would consume it as an alternative to `ghdl/ext:vunit`.

- Is it possible to prepend (not append) args to every vsim, vcom, ghdl call?
- Is it possible to hook commands (to create/kill temporal containers) before and after each 'task'? By 'task' I mean a sequence a commands to be executed in the same container.

A workaround would be to 'hack' it at system level. That is, write some man-in-the-middle shell scripts to get VUnit's calls and forward them to containers. However, it is not clean, because it is not straighforward to handle the lifecycle of tests as VUnit does.

> @kraigher (Gitter):
> @1138-4EB Regarding your use case about customizing the command for running vsim etc. it has some overlap with the use case of running a batch server farm with bsub if you are familiar with that.
> Anyway such things are easier to discuss in an issue rather than in this chat.

issue_title: Suppreress logging of ES connection bodies
issue_body:
Currently, elasticsearch/connection/base.py logs at DEBUG level the body of every request sent to Elasticsearch and every response received back (lines 82, 83, 102, & 107). This is nice in development, but in production, it means that every piece of data that my script sends to or receives from ES - potentially _gigabytes_ of data - is recorded in full in the logs. And, since the entire codebase uses the same logger, I can't turn it off without disabling all DEBUG-level logging for the entire module.

My current workaround is a bit of a hack, creating a new logger, 'elasticsearch.connection', setting it to INFO level, and then manually assigning 'elasticsearch.connection.base.logger' to point to that instead of the orignal.

I propose that something similar be done properly: change line 9 to get a different logger, 'elasticsearch.whatever', in the first place, and document its name, so that users can change the logging level there separately from the rest of the module.

issue_title: Add option to fully synchronise blobs on azure with local folder content
issue_body:
Id like a command to syncronise a container etc to a local directory tree while setting the contentType for all files. Options exist to upload files to plobs in the CLI. This could be an option to the `azure blob upload-batch`

That means it should remove blobs that no longer appear as files in the local directory tree. I have found no such option or other portable CLI tool to do this (not even the old AzCopy). this is critical in applications including a static web site.

The main use case is using blob storage for a static web site and updateing automatically from script for continuous deployment. During rapid iterations the structure can vary and cruft will get left around if blobs are only added. That has a cost impact. This use case also require #1436

With out this option the CD will have to delete all the blobs before uploading. That will require getting a list of blobs, or simply recreating the container, which will lose meta data.

There is also minor security issue with the blobs served over http. Unwanted resources may be addressable when they should not be there at all.

issue_title: Best way to spin up mutliple Kubernetes clusters?
issue_body:
I was approached with the following scenario:

Customer wants to spin up a number of Kubernetes clusters and would like to use the same SSH key for each. He would like to spin these up in parallel for faster deployment.

Option 1
===

Use an ARM template deployment to deploy the clusters in parallel. However, this throws away any convenience introduced by the ACS create command, including the ability to generate SSH keys if not found.  Thus, either the customer would need to manually generate the keys and pass them into as template parameters, or the `group deployment` commands would need to replicate the convenience logic to auto-generate the SSH keys.

Option 2
===

You can start multiple create scenarios using the `--no-wait` flag and then use matching `wait` commands to wait until all of the creates are finished.  This lets you leverage all of the conveniences of the ACS create command, include `--generate-ssh-keys`.

Any insights into better ways to accomplish this scenario?

issue_title: az network dns zone create can overwrite existing zones
issue_body:
Currently, 'az network dns zone create' has an option --if-none-match.

WITH the option, overwrite of existing zones is prevented (via If-None-Match * etag check header)
WITHOUT the option, the command can overwrite an existing zone (but does not override existing records in that zone).

This matches the REST API behaviour, but I don't think it's very intuitive. I don't see a scenario for being able to create a zone when one already exists. I suggest removing the parameter and simply *always* including the If-None-Match * check. I.e. the command can only create a new zone and always fails if the zone already exists.

issue_title: Help.py files should handle/sanitize/not crash with non-ASCII unicode characters
issue_body:
Fact of life: we ask non-developers to submit updates for help docs. We have to assume that, not being Python developers:

1. They aren't Git ninjas
2. Likely aren't using an IDE but something like Github's in-browser editor
3. Will definitely use copious amounts of copy/paste

This is a recipe for disaster. Copy/paste does, with startling frequency, dump evil Unicode characters into the text that the CLI loves to crash on. Then trying to debug and fix these issues somehow results in making a simple help change more work than an actual functional code change.

Sooooo... we need to make the help system more resilient to non-ASCII characters.

issue_title: django tests.py is not handled properly
issue_body:
Issue in #88 introduced a minor bug.

Django creates "tests.py" as the default test file name when you run `./manage.py startapp` and now pre-commit-hooks doesn't handle it properly, seeking only files with test_*.py

Since the last fix added os.path.basename, we're always working with the filename, and should return to using test.*.py

Additionally - readme.md still references test*.py as well, and not test_*.py

The ultra-best-right way to handle this would be to handle it how python does it. unittest.TestLoader.discover does it by allowing any pattern, defaulting to test.py when no argument is provided.

https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/unittest/loader.py 
    def discover(self, start_dir, pattern='test*.py', top_level_dir=None):

issue_title: pybullet visualization with baselines or tensorforce
issue_body:
I am trying to make the pybullet gym environments work with baselines or tensorforce, which works fine so far except for the vizualization. In pybullet, visualization can only be enabled very early on, so that it is initialized together with the physics server, whereas in baselines or tensorforce, you only know that the user wants visualization at the time the library calls render() on the environment.

Is it very hard to make pybullet initialize rendering after initialization of the physics world? In fact it should not be so hard to just read out all physics objects and add visualizations to them. 

For now, the sub-optimal solution to this is just to make pybullet try to connect to a running instance of the GUI and if there is none running, just run headless. However, this makes the visualize flag quite useless in both libraries.

issue_title: btMultiBody depends on bullet3 in debug version
issue_body:
The debug version of bullet 2.x causes a linker error unless bullet3 is also linked in. Unlike in release versions, the "if (0)" clause isn't optimized out at https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/8e163c984ddd0227ae8d901ea3a1a2b99ed1b2fc/src/BulletDynamics/Featherstone/btMultiBody.cpp#L850 and so b3Printf is still required.

You could use "#if 0" and "#endif" to comment out the b3Printf statement if that's the intention.

For some other experiments I am doing with pybullet I wanted to ask if there is some access to collision filtering I am not aware of. So If I load two robots into one world, but do not make them collide with each other, I am under the impression that I can do faster simulation than if I load them in one after the other (maybe I should verify that at some time). Anyhow, I wanted to do the same in pybullet, but did not see any method to do this, correct? Or is there a way to use multiple physics servers at the same time in pybullet and quickly switch my ExampleBrowser view from one to the other?

X is a vector of samples, and I got a trained model from it. I make a copy of this model because I want to do some operations with this copy (like modify the order of the means, or the startprob, etc).  I create a new model from scratch and setup the model as you see in the code below. The problem is when I try to do a prediction of my X vector:

```python
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=DeprecationWarning)
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=RuntimeWarning)
from hmmlearn.hmm import GaussianHMM
import numpy as np

#samples:
X = np.array([[-1.03573482, -1.03573482],
       [ 6.62721065, 11.62721065],

       [ 4.27000819, -1.72999181],
       [ 4.02692237, -1.97307763],
       [ 5.7222677 , 10.7222677 ]])``


# Trainning a new model over samples:
model = GaussianHMM(n_components=3, covariance_type="diag").fit(X)

# Create a new copy of the trained model: 
new_model = GaussianHMM(n_components=3, covariance_type="diag")
new_model.startprob_ = model.startprob_
new_model.transmat_ = model.transmat_ 
new_model.means_ = model.means_
new_model.covars_ = model.covars_

# Predict from X:
X_N, Z_N = new_model.predict(X) 
```
The predict function gives me the following error:
``` python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 2, in <module>
  File "\Python\Python36\site-packages\hmmlearn\base.py", line 334, in predict
    _, state_sequence = self.decode(X, lengths)
  File "\Python36\site-packages\hmmlearn\base.py", line 294, in decode
    self._check()
  File "\Python36\site-packages\hmmlearn\hmm.py", line 175, in _check
    self.n_components)
  File "\lib\site-packages\sklearn\mixture\gmm.py", line 763, in _validate_covars
    raise ValueError("'diag' covars must have shape "
ValueError: 'diag' covars must have shape (n_components, n_dim)
```

It seems like there is a problem in the 'covars' array due to the covariance_type. But since both model has 'covariance_type' = 'diag'. I don't realize why this error exists? Could you help me to fix this error.

## Pre release

## Release

- [ ] Send release mail to google groups

## Long story short

I get a deprecation warning in aiohttp 2.2.5 which mess up Vim's quickfix window

## Expected behaviour

No deprecation warning

## Actual behaviour

The code seems to work but i get this deprecation warning each time i run my tests.
```
/usr/local/opt/Python-3.5.2/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/helpers.py:139: DeprecationWarning: ClientSession.close() is a coroutine
  warnings.warn(self._msg, DeprecationWarning)
```
My tests are based on the unittest package from Python stdlib.

## Steps to reproduce

<!-- Please describe steps to reproduce the issue.
     If you have a script that does that please include it here within
     markdown code markup -->
I run tests using AioHTTPTestCase and unittest_run_loop from aiohttp.test_utils

I don't have any line that close any session in my tests so i guess that the warning comes from the aiohttp library.

## Your environment

<!-- Describe the environment you have that lead to your issue.
     This includes project version, OS, proxy server and other bits that
     are related to your case. -->
```bash
% pip3 freeze | grep aio                
aio-pika==0.18.0
aiodns==1.1.1
aiohttp==2.2.5
```
I'm running Python 3.5.2 using async and await syntax.

This bug is just a marker to let other people know that this is an issue, but not in aiohttp...so if they google for this issue they can find what the real issue is.

Per my research in https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/3933 I found that the underlying Python SSL sockets are leaking.  I logged http://bugs.python.org/issue29870 where they recently found and proposed a patch to fix this leak.  I'll close this as soon as they're released new versions of python with the fix.

I'm not sure if it's worthwhile adding a warning to let users know that certain python versions will leak when using SSL sockets.

issue_title: Server request's state is not cloned.
issue_body:
`req.clone()` call doesn't clone request state, changes made by `req['key'] = val` are not propagated to clone.

The solution is calling `req._state.copy()` in `clone`

issue_title: Websocket compression for send_bytes on by default
issue_body:
## Long story short

I recently upgraded from Aiohttp 2.2.5 to 3.0.6. After that, I got a lot of dropped websocket connections. Through investigation, I found that 2.3.0 introduced the `compression=True` parameter for websockets. It turns out, that this change caused the dropped connections. Simply setting `compression=False` (and thereby returning to the pre-2.3.0 behaviour) solved the issue.

I can understand that having compression on by default makes a lot of sense for text messages (e.g., `send_str` or `send_json` messages). We can naturally assume statistical redundancy for text messages. 
However, I find it strange to find compression on by default for binary messages (e.g., `send_bytes` messages). I don't think we can assume anything about binary messages. Said messages may very well be near-random (and therefore compress very poorly).

In my application, I send a lot of binary data over a websocket (roughly 500 KBps) that doesn't compress very well at all. The (needless) compression caused the python process take up all CPU resources and, ultimately, drop the connection due to timeout.

## Expected behaviour

I expected `send_bytes` to have compression off by default.

## Actual behaviour

Since 2.3.0, `WebSocketResponse` has compression on by default. Since 3.0.0, it is possible to disable compression on a per-method basis (`send_bytes(..., compression=False)`). However, compression is *on* by default, which I think is very questionable.
See https://github.com/aio-libs/aiobotocore/issues/161 for examples

issue_title: pip installed builds of notebook ignore conda environments; calls kernel from root env
issue_body:
I ran into weird situations where I was installing modules and not finding them available in the notebook. 

I suspected something environment related. But when I checked pip from inside the notebook, it didn't line up with any of what was returned when I `pip list`ed from the terminal in my environments. I thought to uninstall notebook from the root environment, thinking that would stop the `jupyter-notebook` command from being intercepted by an overeager path. That did nothing to change the situation, except that once I opened a notebook, and tried import notebook, it would tell me that it had no notebook module. From that I surmised that it was correctly executing the command, but somehow when activating the kernel, it's going to the wrong place.

So I uninstalled ipykernel from my root environment so that if it was trying to grab the kernel from there it would just fail. 

And lo, so it did.

But if this has been an issue for a while, why hadn't I noticed it by now. I'd been using the notebook a lot while I was writing my dissertation. That made me think that it was an issue on master but nothing else.

So, just to check the pervasiveness of this, I created 5 new environments: one environment that uses the notebook from conda (which is still on 4.2.3), 4 environments where I installed the notebook using pip: one editable install on master, and then one of each 4.3, 4.2.3, 4.2.0. 

On all of the pip installs I get the error message

    ~/anaconda3/bin/python3: No module named ipykernel

when it should be calling python from 

    ~/anaconda3/envs/testing_env/bin/python

What was weird was that I have no trouble accessing my ipython kernels as long as I install the notebook using conda. Somehow, it seems to get the correct `python` from the correct environment with access to the correct set of modules as long as you built the notebook with `conda install`.

Remembering that I had recently updated pip to 9.0.1, thinking that that was a plausible commond cause. But it turns out that this is a problem using both pip v9.0.1 and pip v8.1.2.

The one additional thought that I had was that perhaps conda was doing something different with how it was handling path resolution which was appropriately changed in the conda release of the notebook but not in the pip release. Thus it's not an interaction with pip (per se) but an interaction between the fact that I'm trying to use conda as a virtual environment manager and both pip and conda as package managers. But that also didn't sound quite right and I'm not sure how/if to pursue that further.

```xml

                    <action type="shell_command">cd htslib-1.2.1 && make bgzip tabix</action>
                    <action type="move_file">
                        <source>htslib-1.2.1/bgzip</source>
                        <destination>$INSTALL_DIR/bin</destination>
                    </action>
                    <action type="move_file">
                        <source>htslib-1.2.1/tabix</source>
                        <destination>$INSTALL_DIR/bin</destination>
                    </action>
...
```

When run via Galaxy, `` <action type="shell_command">cd htslib-1.2.1 && make bgzip tabix</action>`` has no lasting change on the current directory.

Currently via ``planemo dependency_script`` use of  ``cd htslib-1.2.1`` is never reverted (so the later steps break).

Workaround:

```xml
 <action type="shell_command">cd htslib-1.2.1 && make bgzip tabix && cd ..</action>
```

Charms need to be able to validate their configuration, going into a blocked state until the operator fixes invalid configuration.

charms.reactive should make this easy.

The PostgreSQL charm does this using an `@preflight` mechanism. The hook validates the configuration, and if it fails, sets the blocked state and does sys.exit(0). I do not think this is a good approach, because of the sys.exit(0) hack to abort the process.

You can't do this with standard reactive handlers, because during a config-changed hook handlers may attempt to run using invalid configuration before the config validation handler has had a chance to run.

I do not have an elegant solution to propose.

issue_title: Crash in storage.py if user deleted accounts directory
issue_body:
Reported at https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/storage-py-nonetype-found/2529

If a user deletes the entire `accounts` directory, `storage.py` gives a crash on next run.

Deleting `accounts` without deleting the associated certs is not a recommended action (effectively it creates a referential integrity problem between the two), but maybe `storage.py` should be more robust and can give a more specific and useful error. I'll also file a related issue about how to change e-mail addresses, which in this case is what the user was trying to do.

issue_title: Provide more useful error message when server is in maintenance
issue_body:
I just tried to manually create a cert using the instructions from https://eblog.damia.net/2015/12/03/lets-encrypt-automation-on-debian/

Got this error because of "Certificate issuance temporarily disabled", see https://letsencrypt.status.io/

````
An unexpected error occurred:
The server experienced an internal error :: Error creating new cert
Please see the logfiles in /var/log/letsencrypt for more details.
````

Some minutes later this changed to
````
An unexpected error occurred:
ClientError: <Response [502]>
````

Apparently, I was right in the middle when the downtime started.
From the log:

````
2015-12-15 16:11:17,191:DEBUG:requests.packages.urllib3.connectionpool:"GET /directory HTTP/1.1" 502 1488
2015-12-15 16:11:17,194:DEBUG:root:Received <Response [502]>. (snip)
````

https://acme-v01.api.letsencrypt.org/acme/new-authz :
````
502
Maintenance Time

Sorry! We're doing some cleanup at the moment. We'll be back shortly.
````

If possible, the client should check first if the server is available and report something like "The server is down, please check https://letsencrypt.status.io and try again later".

For various purposes (such as scripting or our own integration tests!) people will sometimes want to run the client in non-interactive mode.  In such situations, it is a mistake to block on user input of any sort.  We should offer a clear cli flag that causes the client to stop asking the user questions.  

In non-interactive mode it may be acceptable to error out if some instruction is missing, though if at all possible that should be deterministic and happen immediately when the client is run, not partway through execution.

issue_title: How to consume tombstone messages with Consumer.poll()?
issue_body:
Description
===========
Consumer.poll() does not seem to return messages that have a key but no value. My application needs to know when a value is deleted. How can I make poll() return these messages, is there configuration I can set?

How to reproduce
================
Use Producer.produce() to send a message that has a key but no value (for a compacted topic). Try to Consume using Consumer.poll().

Checklist

issue_title: httpbakery: discharge does not work when hostname does not contain a dot
issue_body:
When the authentication URL hostname has no dots in, the bakery client makes a cookie with that hostname as the domain, but that's ignored by the Python cookie jar because the domain isn't a FQDN, so the discharge-required (macaraq) flow will not work with local hostnames.

The standard Python behaviour in this case seems to be to add a ".local" suffix to the domain name when adding the cookie to the cookie jar, so it seems that the bakery should do that to fix the issue.

issue_body:
Here is a code which for demonstration returns an RPC error. On the next run connect() should return False and we should keep calling connect() until it returns True. But I noticed when connect returns False, calling it again will cause an exception. Run the following code, and run it again, it never gets to  `print ("it won't get here!")` because it crashes on line `result = client.connect()`

```
from telethon.tl.types.input_peer_channel import InputPeerChannel
from telethon.tl.functions.messages.forward_messages import ForwardMessagesRequest
from telethon.helpers import generate_random_long
from telethon.tl.types.input_peer_chat import InputPeerChat
from telethon.telegram_client import TelegramClient


client = TelegramClient(...)
result = client.connect()
print (result)

if not result: 
    print ("here")
    result = client.connect()
    print ("it won't get here!")
    print (result)

source_chat_id=1234 
source_access_hash=1234
source_chat = InputPeerChannel(source_chat_id, source_access_hash)

#testGroup: 
dest_chat_id = 123400
dest_chat = InputPeerChat(dest_chat_id)

result = client.invoke(ForwardMessagesRequest(from_peer=source_chat, id=[1234], random_id=[generate_random_long()], to_peer=dest_chat))
```

Also I don't understand why there should be history for the connect. If the code crashes why next run remembers the previous crash and connect will return False? Well I understand there might be some RPC connection things like acks going on, but shouldn't that be transparent to end user?

issue_title: topAnswers parameter not working in python-sdk for RnR
issue_body:
Similar to the issue with the [node-js](https://github.com/watson-developer-cloud/node-sdk/commit/7ef790a1612a156b262e16f2963a8bb0b5402ee8) & java sdks, the RnR service's python wrapper doesn't seem to pass the `topAnswers` parameter properly as part of the RetrieveAndRank.rank(...) method (i.e. regardless of what value is passed, the service always returns the default 10).

I believe the fix is to go from  `data = {'answer_metadata': json.dumps({'answers': + top_answers})}` to  `data = {'answers': top_answers}` in the file: https://github.com/watson-developer-cloud/python-sdk/blob/master/watson_developer_cloud/retrieve_and_rank_v1.py

issue_title: Add option to throw validation error on extra keys
issue_body:
I'm not sure if I'm missing something, but I would like to throw error if extra keys are provided, as in:

``` python
>>> class AlbumSchema(Schema):
...     title = fields.Str()

>>> AlbumSchema(strict=True).load({'extra': 2}, allow_extra=False)
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
marshmallow.exceptions.ValidationError: {'_schema': ["Extra arguments passed: ['extra']"]}
```

I've been using the following implementation (taken from https://github.com/sloria/webargs/issues/87#issuecomment-183949558):

``` python
class BaseSchema(Schema):

    @pre_load
    def validate_extra(self, in_data):
        if not isinstance(in_data, dict):
            return

        extra_args = [key for key in in_data.keys() if key not in self.fields]
        if extra_args:
            raise ValidationError('Extra arguments passed: {}'.format(extra_args))
```

I would expect this to be a common need, however, so could it be supported by the library out-of-the-box?

Why the method field is not called when loading a dict?
```python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields

class UserSchema(Schema):
    first_name = fields.Str(load_from='name')
    email = fields.Email()
    custom = fields.Method('yo')

    def yo(self, obj):
        return 'custom'

schema = UserSchema()
result = schema.load({'name': 'j', 'email': 'j@j.jom'})
print result.data # prints {'first_name': u'j', 'email': u'j@j.jom'}   
```

On the `stable` line (`2.15.0`), documentation indicates that `truthy` and `falsy` can be accepted as constructor parameters, but this just isn't true:

```python
>>> import marshmallow
>>> from marshmallow import Schema, fields
>>> class Example(Schema):
...     b = fields.Boolean(truthy=set(['Yes']), falsy=set(['No']))
... 
>>> ex = Example()
>>> print(ex.load({'b': 'Yes'}))
UnmarshalResult(data={}, errors={'b': ['Not a valid boolean.']})
```

To get this behavior in 2.15.0, you need to subclass `fields.Boolean` overriding the `truthy` and `falsy` properties:

```python
>>> from marshmallow import Schema, fields
>>> class MyBoolean(fields.Boolean):
...     truthy = set(['Yes'])
...     falsy = set(['No'])
... 
>>> class Example(Schema):
...     b = MyBoolean()
... 
>>> ex = Example()
>>> print(ex.load({'b': 'Yes'}))
UnmarshalResult(data={'b': True}, errors={})
>>> print(ex.load({'b': 'No'}))
UnmarshalResult(data={'b': False}, errors={})
>>> print(ex.load({'b': 'True'}))
UnmarshalResult(data={}, errors={'b': ['Not a valid boolean.']})
```

In the `dev` line, the behavior matches the documentation (`truthy` and `falsy` are constructor params)

issue_title: Cannot exclude nested fields using dot notation

issue_body:
Loom uses chunked, compressed storage in order to achieve reasonable performance along both axes of a layer. We use 64x64 chunks which we found a good tradeoff. 

However, if we knew that the matrix would only be accessed along the columns (say), then we could store it ordered by columns. This could potentially speed up access by an order of magnitude (or two). But, sadly, access along the other dimension would be *slowed down* by an order of magnitude (or two). For example, Joe Kington [did some benchmarking](http://geology.beer/2015/02/10/hdf-for-large-arrays/) on an  8GB dataset with three dimensions. With chunked storage, reading a slice along any dimension took 20 seconds. Without chunking, this dropped to 0.3 seconds along the favourable dimension, but increased to *3.5 hours* along the unfavourable one. So, this is why we use chunking.

Still, those 0.3 seconds are sure attractive. What if we would store each layer twice in the file, with one copy transposed? When the user slices along one of the dimensions, we would simply read from the appropriate underlying dataset. 

The problem is that we currently allow datasets to grow (by the addition of columns). Adding columns to the column-major dataset would be simple, but adding to the row-major one would be near-impossible.

Hence the proposal for "frozen" Loom files. A frozen file would work the same as a standard Loom file, but would be immutable. Once a file is frozen, it would be impossible to add columns (or rows) to any of the layers. But row and column attributes, and graphs, would still be mutable as before.

Here's an outline of the spec:

* Frozen loom files contain **three** copies of each layer: one chunked just like before, and two continuous, uncompressed. One of the continuous copies is transposed relative to the other. 

* Frozen files are created by freezing an existing file. Once frozen, the file becomes immutable, and cannot be thawed.

Benefits:

* Potentially 10 - 100x faster performance

* Current code would be completely unchanged except for a single call to ds.freeze()

* Backwards compatible, with same performance as before

Drawbacks:

* No compression, and duplication of data, so file size would grow about 5-10-fold. For example, a file currently compressed to 30% would contain the original layer (30% of the dataset size) plus two full-sized copies (200% of the dataset). Thus it would grow from 30% to 230%, or 7.5-fold.

So, basically, you would trade 10-100-fold speed for 5-10-fold file size. Might be a good deal in many cases.

issue_title: Fail on deletion of a stack with traffic
issue_body:
Current behaviour of senza when deleting a stack with 100% traffic is to silently remove the stack and DNS entries, which leads to "unknown host" for all users. This is potentially error-prone and can lead to downtimes in production. It would be better if it does one of the following

* either die and not allow to delete an instance that has (full / any) traffic
* or route traffic to one of the other stacks that already have traffic

Imho, the first proposal makes more sense, and the latter might make sense in case you have e.g. two stacks with traffic.

While trying to deploy with senza `Senza 2.0.121`, the following error appears:
`EXCEPTION OCCURRED: An error occurred (ValidationError) when calling the CreateStack operation: 'null' values are not allowed in templates
Validation Error: 'null' values are not allowed in templates`

Using senza print, the field `SSLCertificateId` is set to `null`, even though the `.yaml` file includes this:
`"Listeners": [
                    {
                        "InstancePort": 8080,
                        "LoadBalancerPort": 443,
                        "PolicyNames": [],
                        "Protocol": "HTTPS",
                        "SSLCertificateId": null
                    }
                ]`

issue_title: Senza version warning should not be printed for non-TTYs (piping result to awk etc)
issue_body:
The Senza version check currently destroys popular shell scripting such as:

```
senza li -o tsv | tail -n +2 | awk '{ print $1 }'
```

We should only print the warning if `sys.stdout` is a TTY.

issue_title: enable connection draining on ELB by default
issue_body: Connection draining is essential to ensure that running requests are finished before an instance is removed on scale down. So it should be enabled by default in the CF created by senza.

Most python packages nowadays can be installed via pip. For z3, this would look like:

```ShellSession
# pip install z3
```

This is super awesome not only because it makes it super easy to install z3, but also because it lets people easily put z3 in the requirements.txt of their packages, so that pip will automatically install it as a dependency, which will be cool beyond belief!

There are two steps required to support this:

1. Create a `setup.py` that installs the package.
2. Register and upload a build to https://pypi.python.org

I'll submit a pull request for 1 shortly. I don't think it's my place to do 2, but once 1 is done, it's a matter of a few commands that you can run to register and upload to pypi.

issue_title: Java API: expr.isInt() seems not to work as expr.isBool() or expr.isBV()
issue_body:
Hi!
Z3, 4.4.2. 
Java API.

I suspect this might be an error:
```java
System.out.println(ctx.mkIntConst("ico").isInt()); 
System.out.println(ctx.mkInt(4).isInt()); 
```
print respectively
```java
false
true
```
which seems not to respect to the corresponding [C++ specification] (http://z3prover.github.io/api/html/classz3_1_1expr.html#ad5f0c356738a1efd67332e7c31aff42c); I am therefore inspecting the return value of getSort() to check if the expression is of Int sort, but I wonder if this was intentional.

Compare with analogous expressions for bitvector or boolean sort:
```java
System.out.println(ctx.mkBoolConst("bco")  .isBool());
System.out.println(ctx.mkBool(true)              .isBool());
System.out.println(ctx.mkBVConst("bvco",9).isBV());
System.out.println(ctx.mkBV(19,9).isBV());
```
print all `true`.

Greetings!

It's probably a small issue in most image-CNN-related cases, but when dealing with text, multi-input NNs, reinforcement learning or long-term memory networks some layers should be applied at multiple spots with same weights.

Some usage cases:
* Text processing: Apply the same embedding and text convolution / recurrent processing to advertisement title and description
* Reinforcement learning: Apply critic subnetwork in deterministic policy graident algorithm should evaluate both "optimal" and actual(i.e. with exploration) actor output.

As far as i understood the docs, one natural way to do so in Lasagne is by passing other layer's params on creation, e.g.

```
l_d0 = DenseLayer(bottom_layer,10)
l_d0_again = DenseLayer(other_bottom_layer, 10,  W = l_d0.W, b = l_d0.b)
```
or alternatively, create several networks and use .get_output
```
lasagne.layers.get_output(my_nn, {my_nn_input:input1}
lasagne.layers.get_output(my_nn, {my_nn_input:input2}
```

The problem with the first approach is that it takes immensely many lines of code to do so with larger networks, especially with e.g. LSTM with a lot more parameters. Not to mention it's easy to make a mistake that way.

The problem with the second approach is that it forces you to break outside lasagne network and makes routine tasks e.g. getting all params, regularizing, applying flags more complicated and verbose.

It may be a good idea to introduce some simpler method to do that.

For example, in [blocks](https://github.com/mila-udem/blocks) layers can be .apply --ed to as many spots as necessary with all params shared.
```
>>> from blocks.bricks import Linear
>>> from blocks.initialization import IsotropicGaussian, Constant
>>> linear = Linear(input_dim=10, output_dim=5,
...                 weights_init=IsotropicGaussian(),
...                 biases_init=Constant(0.01))
>>> y = linear.apply(x)
```
This complicates the code a lot and i personally don't think this is the best solution since most of layers are only applied once.

Another approach is to make some .reapply() method for layers with params that would work like a cloning constructor that reuses the original layer's weights, but that too makes cloning large nets complicated.
```
l_d0 = DenseLayer(bottom_layer)
l_d0_again = l_d0.reapply(other_bottom_layer)
```

In one of our libraries for reinforcement learning we use a generic function to clone a lasagne network with or without keeping the params. 
The source can be found here - https://github.com/yandexdataschool/AgentNet/blob/master/agentnet/utils/clone.py 
Some usage examples - https://github.com/yandexdataschool/AgentNet/blob/master/tests/test_clone_and_targets.py#L45

__The question is:__ is there any way to implement functionality of applying network parts multiple times in Lasagne?

p.s. if our clone_network can fit Lasagne spirit, we'd be glad to contribute the code (or you can just grab it at your will, so far as i understand the license - the code has almost no dependencies but for theano and lasagne)

`fs.path.join` coalesces slashes, which breaks protocol declarations if passed a URL. I'm not sure if it _should_ be URL-aware, but it should probably be consistent with `fs.path.combine`:

```
fs.path.join('smb://user:pass@host/svc','path')
'smb:/user:pass@host/svc/path'
# open_fs will treat this as an OS path

fs.path.combine('smb://user:pass@host/svc','path')
'smb://user:pass@host/svc/path'
# open_fs will treat this as a SMB path
```

issue_title: Table.plot should take column_for_xticks as optional
issue_body: If it is not specified, it should be assumed to be 0:Table.num_rows.  Otherwise it is much harder to do the obvious thing of just plotting the values in a table.  And it is unnecessarily different from matplotlib plot.

```python

xspl = np.linspace(start, end, round((end - start) / self._dlambda)+1)

while I think the user expects bins of 1 nm in size at this point?
My solution would do exactly that.

issue_title: Change default way that `hadd` is used for merging `root` files
issue_body:
I just thought I should let you know about some new options I submitted to ROOT for their hadd application, that have been available since the 6.06.00 release, that I think should be utilized by the merging in ganga.

The release notes are here

https://root.cern.ch/doc/v606/release-notes.html#hadd

but in short it was to

- Add support for the newer LZMA compression
- Add an option to automatically set the merged file compression level, based on what the input files used.

Now, one other thing you should take into account is by default the ROOT tuple files created by DaVinci etc., now use the LZMA level 6 compression level, not the ROOT default which is ZLIB level 1. This was done as it makes quite a nice reduction in the size of the ROOT files the users get back (There is an option in DaVinci to change this if a user really wants, but I guess most do not know it exists, which was partly the idea ;))

The problem is if you just merge files with

> hadd merged.root in1.root in2.root

then merged.root is forced to be ZLIB:1, even if the input is different. This is a problem because it forces ROOT into a much (orders of magnitude) slower mode of operation, as instead of just piping the compressed data stream directly from the input to output, it has to uncompress and recompress it.

To get around this you should use the -ff option, so

> hadd -ff merged.root in1.root in2.root

( I tried my best to argue that this should be the default mode, but the ROOT devs wouldn't go that far...)

So... The point I am trying to get to is what version of ROOT does ganga now internally use for merging ? If it is 6.06.00 or newer, I would strongly recommend using the -ff option for any hadd merging you do internally.

cheers Chris

issue_title: How to manually patch the kernel for INZI?
issue_body:
| Required Info                         |                                                                |
|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------- |
| Camera Model                       | SR300 | 
| Firmware Version                   |  | 
| Operating System & Version |   Debian  | 
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)    |  4.11.0 | 
| Platform                                 | ARM board |
| SDK Version                            |  SDK2  | 


I want to get depth frames and process by ARM board with Debian system. I build `pyrealsense2.so` in my ARM board. Then I run the following code in python:

> import pyrealsense2 as rs
> pipeline = rs.pipeline()
> pipeline.start()

I run into an error: 
> RuntimeError: The requested pixel format 'INZI' is not natively supported by the Linux

According to [dorodnic's answer](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/issues/1785), I can manually patch the kernel or run cmake with `-DFORCE_LIBUVC=true`.

I run cmake with `-DFORCE_LIBUVC=true` but get `Segmentation fault` after several frames. I asked [a question](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/issues/1821) but nobody give solution.

So it seems that run cmake with `-DFORCE_LIBUVC=true` not work for me; therefore, the only solution is manually patch the kernel. I try to follow [your answer](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/issues/1186) @dorodnic : 
```
You will need to add couple lines of code to /drivers/media/usb/uvc/uvc_driver.c, /drivers/media/usb/uvc/uvcvideo.h and /include/uapi/linux/videodev2.h to make V4L2 recognise INZI. You can use the patch for Ubuntu as reference.
Once you make the changes and successfully rebuild the kernel, it would be relatively easy to generate new .patch that you are welcomed to share here for future use.
```

But I do not find `/drivers/media/usb/uvc/uvc_driver.c` or `uvcvideo.h` or `videodev2.h` in my Debian system. There is no `/driver` directory in my system. Do I need to download something? @dorodnic

The [Big List of Naughty Strings](https://github.com/minimaxir/big-list-of-naughty-strings) is a list of strings which have a high probability of causing issues when used as user-input data.

Make sure Pulp is able to deal with these strings.

On #332 we agreed that we are able to drop Python 2 support for Pulp Smash. Here is the plan to actually get that done:

- [ ] Drop the compat module
- [ ] Update the imports to reflect the removal of the compat module
- [ ] Drop unittest2 in favor of Python 3's unitttest module and update the code
- [ ] Drop mock in favor of the builtin Python 3's mock

issue_title: smash tests should remove orphans on cleanup
issue_body:
The removal of each of the items in `cls.resources` is automatic and often includes repositories. If those repos were synced, this will leave orphaned content units. Removing these units between tests is necessary to ensure full clean up.

An example of how to do this is here:
https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/blob/master/pulp_smash/tests/rpm/api_v2/test_orphan_remove.py#L46

Automatic cleanup could be done here:
https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/blob/master/pulp_smash/utils.py#L104

the current ssl lookup is flawed, and managing SSL certificates via IAM is sort of deprecated by AWS.
kiso already supports adding certs to ACM already for a while, so having a lookup makes sense.

I propose the following way to implement it:

* pass a list of hostnames that should be secured.
* check all certificates in ACM if the configured CN ('DomainName') or SANs (`SubjectAlternativeNames`) (including wildcards) if they match for the given list of hostnames
* the chosen certificates `STATUS` must be `ISSUED`
* if there are multiple matches, use the one with the most distant expiry date
* return the ARN of the certificate

issue_title: Openthread platform pin/peripheral configuration is hardcoded
issue_body:
For all platforms, the UART pin/peripheral configurations are hardcoded in the Openthread library. For example  `openthread/examples/platforms/kw41z/uart.c` hardcodes the use of UART0 and the UART pins  as PTC6 and PTC7. This makes it difficult to use the library on any board besides the development kits these choices were based on. Many modern SoCs allow extensive pin/peripheral muxing to allow flexible design, and many projects won't use the same pin configuration as the library currently supports.

Pin/peripheral configuration should be defined in a separate board file that allows customization for various projects.

Currently OT supports Mesh Header with Hops Left field set to 14 or less. However this is not always true, and we need to support Deep Hops Left field. 

Also the algorithm of choosing Hops Left is described in 5.10.1.1 however it is also a little implementation dependent.

I think this field may be set to 14 if calculated (predicted) hop number is less that 14, and set to calculated hop number + X (1-2?) if it's higher than 14. Anyway we should use value 14 or less as much as possible to save extra byte.

Side note: Unfortunately Mesh Header is constructed in a way that Deep Hops Left is injected in the middle of the header, so when we forward a frame and the Hops Left is 15, then we can do memmove of payload to save one byte or just use Deep Hops Left until end (thoughts?)

issue_title: The default imports in __init__ causes significant lag
Dataframe output: Column types depend on the value of default
issue_body:
So the Type of the output column types is the largest class containing all types in every column (typically object)

    check_df = pd.DataFrame({'A': [1.0, 2.0], 'B':[1,2], 'C':['A', 'B' ]})
    mapper_check= skp.DataFrameMapper([('A', preprocessing.LabelBinarizer())], default=False, df_out=True)
    mapper_check.fit_transform(check_df).dtypes
    A    int64
    dtype: object

now use default= None

    mapper_check= skp.DataFrameMapper([('A', preprocessing.LabelBinarizer())], default=None, df_out=True)
    mapper_check.fit_transform(check_df).dtypes
    A    object
    B    object
    C    object
    dtype: object

So as we see incorporating the default = None changes the type of column A. This is due to the fact, that the stacked arrays only have one type.

So a fix would be to check first if df_out is true and then construct the stacked arrays

issue_title: Support new SparkPost A/B testing option
issue_body:
SparkPost has recently introduced [transactional A/B testing](https://www.sparkpost.com/docs/tech-resources/a-b-testing-sparkpost/), which automates comparing performance on transactional template variations.

If and when the [python-sparkpost](https://github.com/SparkPost/python-sparkpost) package is updated to support the new `ab_test_id` content option, it will become usable through Anymail's [`esp_extra`](https://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/stable/esps/sparkpost/#esp-extra-support) property.  (Until then, there's not really any way for Anymail to expose the feature, short of dropping python-sparkpost and writing directly to the SparkPost REST API.)

issue_title: How to create a type alias of a vector<T>?
issue_body:
I'm looking for a method to construct a simple type alias, which I couldn't find explained in the manual/examples.

Say I have `std::vector<T>`, how can I register it in my module named `foo` under the name `foo.Vector`? This didn't work for me:

```cpp
  py::class_<std::vector<T>>(m, "Vector")
    .def(py::init<>());
```

When I try to call `foo.Vector([])`, I get this error:
```
TypeError: Incompatible function arguments. The following argument types are supported:
    1. (list<foo.T>) -> NoneType
     Invoked with: <foo.Vector object at 0x10ea1c688>, []
```

(Type `T` is just a placeholder for another type announced via `py::class_<T>(m, "...")`.)

`pybind11.get_include()` does not return where the pybind11 headers are actually installed. It returns where pip would have installed by default.

To reproduce:
```
pip3 install --install-option="--prefix=/tmp/pyb" .
```
and then run
```
export PYTHONPATH=/tmp/pyb/lib/python3.5/site-packages/
python3 -c "import pybind11; print(pybind11.get_include()); print(pybind11.get_include(True))"
```

issue_title: Typeid information not synchronized across shared library boundaries
issue_body:
Dear all,

I've been running into strange inconsistencies where type information is not synchronized across shared library boundaries. This happens when using Clang & libc++ on Linux/x86_64 machines.

Before escalating to Clang/LLVM, I wanted to gauge if anyone here has previously run into this. To reproduce:

issue.py
```
import mod1, mod2

mod2.test(mod1.Test())

# Expected output
# ================
# $ python issue.py
# $


# Actual output:
# ================
# $ python issue.py
# Traceback (most recent call last):
#   File "issue.py", line 3, in <module>
#     mod2.test(mod1.Test())
# TypeError: test(): incompatible function arguments. The following argument types are supported:
#     1. (arg0: Test) -> None
#
# Invoked with: <mod1.Test object at 0x7f12861f5df8>
```

CMakeLists.txt:
```cmake
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 2.8.12)
project(example)

# Issue is triggered in a release build
set(CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE Release CACHE STRING "" FORCE)

# Issue is triggered when using libc++
set(CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS "${CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS} -stdlib=libc++")

add_subdirectory(pybind11)

add_library(shared SHARED shared.cpp)

pybind11_add_module(mod1 mod1.cpp)
pybind11_add_module(mod2 mod2.cpp)

target_link_libraries(mod1 shared)
target_link_libraries(mod2 shared)
```

shared.h
```cpp
#pragma once

#define EXPORT __attribute__ ((visibility("default")))

class EXPORT Test {
public:
    Test();
};
```

shared.cpp
```cpp
#include "shared.h"

Test::Test() { }
```

mod1.cpp
```cpp
#include <pybind11/pybind11.h>
#include "shared.h"

namespace py = pybind11;

PYBIND11_MODULE(mod1, m) {
    py::class_<Test>(m, "Test")
        .def(py::init<>());
}
```

mod2.cpp
```cpp
#include <pybind11/pybind11.h>
#include "shared.h"

namespace py = pybind11;

void test(Test b) { }

PYBIND11_MODULE(mod2, m) {
    py::module::import("mod1");
    m.def("test", &test);
}
```

Best,
Wenzel

issue_body:
khal affected as well.

https://github.com/codecov/support/issues/194

Our documentation uses "certificate" and "cert" interchangeably. For better or worse, many of our flag names do too. We should fix the documentation and the `--help` output to consistently use certificate instead of cert.

Particularly post http-01 but also just generally post-release, we now know more about what people need from IPv6 support in Nginx.

From https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/5438:

> The final case is when searching the config to see if there any IPv6 listens, we ignore (to different degrees) the address and port. We completely ignore the address and for the port, we're only looking for ipv6only. Even if nginx isn't listening with IPv6 on that port, we'll still use IPv6 here because it's listening on IPv6 for some address/port combination.
> 
> This is not likely to be a problem for (m)any Certbot users, but some people with valid setups have specific requirements for what addresses and ports a services should be listening to. For example, they may have multiple IP addresses allocated to a box with different services running on each. Certbot configuring nginx to use additional addresses/ports beyond what is currently in the nginx config or specified by the user can cause the challenge to fail. (It also will likely make the challenge succeed in other cases it wouldn't previously.)
> 

I think we should do something like what we do for `ssl` for detection -- on `get_hosts`, update ipv6 info based on what we now know from parsed `ipv6only=on` parameters.

                        certificate, specifies the new certificate's name.
                        (default: None)
```

Thank you.

issue_title: certbot http -> https rewrite rules messes up GET params
issue_body:
Hello,

An issue I encountered today: the rewrite rules added to apache config by certbot-auto to coerce http requests to https doesn't seem to be bulletproof.

In particular, GET params that are url-encoded, eg ?email=testest%2Byhbugfixer%40gmail.com, seem to end up getting encoded twice so that my application receives 'testest%2Byhbugfixer%40gmail.com' for the param value instead of the intended 'testest+yhbugfixer@gmail.com', if I make the initial request in http://

Notably, this does not happen if in place of the rewrite rules added by certbot-auto, I simply create a new virtual host like so:

 <VirtualHost *:80>
        ServerName mydomain.com
        Redirect / https://mydomain.com/
 </VirtualHost>

## My operating system is CENTOS7
## I installed Certbot with OS package manager
## I ran this command and it produced this output:

> certbot certonly --manual --preferred-challenges dns --dry-run -d <my_domain> --manual-auth-hook renew_auth.sh

My renew_auth.sh successfully integrates the $CERTBOT_VALIDATION into <my_domain> TXT record.

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
Additionally to TXT I have a CAA record for <my_domain> saying:

> <my_domain>     rdata_257 = 128 issue "Let's Encrypt Authority X3"

which is perfect for A+ validating <my_domain> with ssslabs.com and to me it looks great.

But then I get 

> -- The following errors were reported by the server:
>   Domain: <my_domain>
>   Type:   connection
>   Detail: CAA record for <my_domain> prevents issuance

## Here is a Certbot log showing the issue (if available): same as above

From my point of view a valid CAA record explaining lets encrypt as certificate authority is
a building block in ssl confidence and validation and should not interfere and **prevent** with renewal of my
certificate.

dirty workaround: remove CAA record and add it again after renewal

issue_title: Unable to install certficiate to Apache
issue_body:
I tried to obtain and install a certificate for Apache today and while I was able to obtain one, I wasn't able to install it. Apart from the fact that the command in the beta invite e-mail was using auth which is afaik not installing the certificate and you can't simply say “okay now install it”.

Anyway I got the following error:

    Cannot find a cert or key directive in
    /files/etc/apache2/sites-available/ssl.conf/virtualhost.
    VirtualHost was not modified

And I'm wondering what exactly that means. Now looking at the [source code](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/letsencrypt-apache/letsencrypt_apache/configurator.py#L203) it seems that `ssl.conf` already needs to contain `SSLCertificateFile` and `SSLCertificateKeyFile` which seem odd because if they are already there… I wouldn't need to install it.

Now for full disclosure, I already had tried to use SSL so the file already had `SSLCertificateFile` defined (but not the `SSLCertificateKeyFile`). So maybe that's the issue?

issue_title: Issue with MAVProxy 'position' command in GUIDED mode
issue_body:
When operating in GUIDED mode, issuing the command 'position x y z' invokes the cmd_position()
method in [mavproxy_cmdlong.py](https://github.com/ArduPilot/MAVProxy/blob/f35a9837f1d6861fd558c9f90171ac6e2af8e251/MAVProxy/modules/mavproxy_cmdlong.py#L229-L249)

Observe these lines of the function in particular:

```
            self.master.mav.set_position_target_local_ned_send(
                                      0,  # system time in milliseconds
                                      1,  # target system
                                      0,  # target component
                                      8,  # coordinate frame MAV_FRAME_BODY_NED
                                      3576,     # type mask (pos only)
                                      x_m, y_m, z_m,  # position x,y,z
                                      0, 0, 0,  # velocity x,y,z
                                      0, 0, 0,  # accel x,y,z
                                      0, 0)     # yaw, yaw rate
                                      0, 0, 0,  # accel x,y,z
                                      0, 0)     # yaw, yaw rate
```
Why is 'target system' hardcoded to 1 in cmd_position() (and cmd_velocity() for that matter), when all other command definitions use this.settings.target_system instead? The consequence I have observed in not using 'this.settings.target_system' is that MAVProxy's 'position' and 'setspeed' commands are simply not relayed to an autopilot with a SYSID_THIS_MAV parameter of greater than 1.

I have resolved the issue through replacing '1' with 'this.settings.target_system' in my own local copy of mavproxy_cmdlong.py and have eliminated the issue.

The following settings could be added to the schedule section. The following defaults are in effect for each:

            email_on_retry=True,
            email_on_failure=True,
            retries=0,
            retry_delay=timedelta(seconds=300),
            retry_exponential_backoff=False,
            max_retry_delay=None,

## Problem Definition

When the external endpoint changes, we need to change our entry in the registry. 

## Solution

We should regularly check if our registered address still corresponds to our physical location. If it has changed ("roaming"), we need to also check if our port-mapping (UPnP/STUN) is still valid.

Note that there are some conditions that need to be handled (if even possible):
- we may need to re-establish our port mapping
- we may have roamed into a non-mappable  NAT profile (i.e. from `restricted` to `symmetric`)
- we may have roamed into a local area network without internet access

issue_title: Force the opening party to do a deposit
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

A channel is only considered open after the first deposit, this is adding complexity in the code base to handle initializing channels, forcing the party creating the channel to deposit is a nice way to fix this. At the same time, this is a nice optimization, reducing the number of transactions required to open a channel from 2 to 1, and makes more sense, since the party willing to deposit in the channel should be the one creating it, not the other way around.

## Tasklist

- [ ] change the channel manager to also do a token deposit after the channel is created
- [ ] remove the notion of initializing channel from the the code base

## Related

https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/517

issue_title: Wrong token range given for single-item list with trailing comma
issue_body:
Hello,

Great library! I've been using it to implement [this pre-commit hook](https://github.com/LiraNuna/py-ensure-trailing-commas) that ensures trailing commas exist.

Here is a simple repro case to illustrate this behavior:

```python
import ast, asttokens


class TestcaseVisitor(ast.NodeVisitor):
    def __init__(self, atok):
        self.atok = atok

    def visit_List(self, node):
        super().generic_visit(node)

        tokens = self.atok.token_range(node.first_token, node.last_token, include_extra=True)
        for token in tokens:
            print(token)


atok = asttokens.ASTTokens("""
[
    1,
]
""", parse=True)
visitor = TestcaseVisitor(atok)
visitor.visit(atok.tree)
```

This will output 

```
OP:'['
NL:'\n'
NUMBER:'1'
```

Where the expected result should be

```
OP:'['
NL:'\n'
NUMBER:'1'
OP:','
NL:'\n'
OP:']'
```

Please note that removing the trailing comma OR adding a second element to the list will not reproduce the bug.

Hello everybody,

I stumbled upon the following issue while implementing advanced filtering in the admin views (using [advanced-filters](https://github.com/modlinltd/django-advanced-filters)):
Polymorphic models can't be filtered, because the `Q` object passed from *django-advanced-filters* is not interpreted correctly (or the `Q` passed from *django-advanced-filters* does not conform to standards):

```
AttributeError
'list' object has no attribute 'children'
/home/till/.virtualenvs/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/polymorphic/query_translate.py  in tree_node_correct_field_specs, line 59
```

The error occurs in [`query_translate.py:59`](https://github.com/till89/django-polymorphic/blob/v1.3/polymorphic/query_translate.py#L59) when trying to iterate over what are presumed to be nested `Q`'s - `node` in my problematic context is a list, but the query translation assumes it to be another `Q` object. This "recursion" is caused by [this](https://github.com/till89/django-polymorphic/blob/v1.3/polymorphic/query_translate.py#L62) (excerpt below):

```
if type(child) == tuple:
    # a kwarg like tuple
else:
    # child is another Q object
```

In my case, the `Q` is neither a tuple *nor* a `Q`, and thus the control flow fails.

I am not sure whether this is something that should be fixed in *django-advanced-filters* or here, in *django-polymorphic* (or actually any where at all), but I found a very easy fix for my problem by simply changing the check for a tuple to include a list:

```
if isinstance(child, (tuple, list)):
     # a kwarg like tuple
else:
    # child is another Q object
``` 

Because I am not sure whether that is on purpose or not, I have not yet created an official pull request (the full code is [here](https://github.com/till89/django-polymorphic/tree/feature/q-translation-list)).

issue_title: Inline formset fields have 'required' attribute (Django 1.10 issue)
issue_body:
When using ``polymorphic_inlineformset_factory()`` it seems that the resulting forms have the ``required`` HTML attribute set, even though they should actually be optional.

I tracked this as far as the ``Form.use_required_attribute``, [which was added in Django 1.10](https://github.com/django/django/commit/ec6121693f112ae33b653b4364e812722d2eb567). This is now set in Django's own formset code, but isn't set by django-polymorphic.

However, I couldn't quite figure out how to actually fix this. I also only have so much time available so unfortunately I need to move on. I hope that this will help someone find a solution.

Right now when we open a notebook with pending jobs, the status of tasks remains at the time when the notebook is closed. It would be better to update them when the notebook is opened. This would also allow us to start or stop a task.

```
Warning in 2:K :
  numerical expression has 2 elements: only the first used
Error in 2:K : NA/NaN argument
```

But SoS will keep running, not reporting the error at this stage. I guess it is saving it for later.

Separate from #952 , this ticket keeps discussion on some thoughts on SoS Data Exchanger. The general idea is to use a neutral interface for all languages, with implementation details broken down to various functions that can be added incrementally in time. This is different design from using some intermediate common file formats, say JSON or HDF5, where there will be loss of information and performance issues.

issue_title: AWG 5014 ref clock bad parameter name /confusion
issue_body:
The AWG 5014 driver has two distinct "parameters" in the hardware, 
- Clock source -> int or ext 
- Reference oscillator -> int or ext
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6142932/24244655/209c178c-0fc0-11e7-8f63-8ca102bf1acf.png)


The recently updated 5014 driver introduces a 'parameter' ref_clock_source. 
https://github.com/QCoDeS/Qcodes/blob/master/qcodes/instrument_drivers/tektronix/AWG5014.py#L160
This refers to using an internal or external clock and and has nothing to do with the reference oscillator, making this a confusing name. 
Additionally there is no parameter that sets the reference oscillator. 

The driver does contain some reference to these parameter under their proper name. 
https://github.com/QCoDeS/Qcodes/blob/master/qcodes/instrument_drivers/tektronix/AWG5014.py#L847-L852

I would suggest the following fix. 
- Rename the `ref_clock_source` parameter to `clock_source`. 
- Add a parameter for the `reference_source`
 Both of these names are the names that the AWG uses internally, both in it's GUI as in it's sequencer config. 

@WilliamHPNielsen as author of the recent fix, do you agree with these changes? If so I will be sending a PR shortly.

Relayed from @spauka :
When developing a driver, reloading a module doesn't update existing objects; this is standard Python behavior. This means that the old Instrument object stays around with the old definition, and the natural way to get to the new state is to stop and restart Python.
It would be nice if there were a way to easily find and delete the old Instrument object while reloading the module.

I'm specifically concerned about jupyter-notebook and ipython shell since I'd like to avoid reinstallation of these two, as well as a virtualenv.

issue_title: Issues with Reports coverage and functionality
issue_body:
Version: 0.10.0
Python version: 3.7

**1) Available Account Reports do not have IDs in Canvas**

In Canvas, the list of available reports do not have IDs. 

An available report has these fields: report, title, parameters
A report has these fields: id, report, file_url, attachment, status, created_at, started_at, ended_at, parameters, progress, current_line

In Account.py I see that there is a class for AccountReport. I wonder if there should be a class for AccountReport and another class called AvailableAccountReport or something similar? And then get_reports() would return a PaginatedList of type AvailableAccountReport.

I noticed this when doing the following:
```
reports = account.get_reports()

for report in reports:
    print(report)
```
The above code gave me this error:
```AttributeError: 'AccountReport' object has no attribute 'id'```
This is my workaround:
```
reports = account.get_reports()

for report in reports:
    print(report.attributes)
```

**2) Unless I missed it, the wrapper is missing most Report methods** 
I need functionality to start a report and get the status of a report. It looks like this isn't supported in the wrapper? 


Let me know what you think and I can create a fork with some fixes if you'd like.

Getting user enrollments requires a call to get the user (which pulls their profile info).  User enrollments and User profile use two different access roles.  An admin CAN'T pull the user profile of another admin, but CAN pull the enrollments of that admin.

user = canvas.get_user(user_id)
courses = user.get_courses(enrollment_type='Teacher', include=['sections'])

It would be great if I can setup a fake user object with the user id so that I can then reference the enrollments.

setup: ray 0.2.0 on a centos 7 cluster of 10 machines (56 cores, 256GB ram each, glusterfs), all stock ray parameters.

I have noticed extremely slow times at the end of a remote function returning a dict (which is in the 5-10 MB range).  I haven't tested different sizes.  It takes minutes to return.  Or more specifically I can see that the ray.put(dict) of this size in the routine causes that much delay.  

To test instead of returning the dict I pickled it to our glusterfs and picked it up in the returning function and unpickled it and that was very fast. (ray return well over a minute, pickle/unpickle to filesystem closer to 1 second.)

This seems like a serious issue as I don't want to pickle to disk whenever returning an object of any non-trivial size.

thoughts? I am not sure how to instrument more or how to perhaps log more in the ray infrastructure but am happy to test various things.

Is this perhaps related to https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/ARROW-1282 ?

thanks,
Luke

A common pattern for a Ray process is to connect to some socket(s). In these cases, we often try once and exit violently if the initial connection is unsuccessful. However, these failures to connect may be transient (for instance, if the `listen` buffer on the server-side is full during `photon_connect`). In these cases, we should retry the connection, with some backoff.

Maybe the path should also reflect build type and version numbers?
The question is what should happen if no version is known or the build type is Default or wasn't specified...
- For unknown versions, instead of a folder like `v1.0.0` maybe `vAny`?
- For unspecified/default build type, maybe folder `Default`?

That aside, it would make sense to have paths such as `build/release/targetname/1.0.0/` (with subdirectories for external_sources, obj/source_subfolder, lib, bin, include and possibly others).

Furthermore, one could add a `package` flag to targets, which could be used to gather binaries and includes for a target and place them in corresponding folders, e.g. build/package/targetname/1.0.0/bin. Instead of a `package` flag, it could also be a build type (I would always want to create the package, but some may only want to do this when a release is published?).

So:
- build
  - release
    - targetname
      - 1.0.0
        - external_sources
        - obj
        - depfiles
        - lib
        - bin
        - include
      - 1.1.0
        - ...
  - debug
    - ...
  - package
    - targetname
      - 1.0.0
      - 1.1.0

issue_title: Option to keep tuples on the same line
issue_body:
I'm using rapidjson (great package, thank you!) to dump contents to a json file, and I use `indent=0` to get a readable/diffable file.

However I have many tuples in my files, such as transformation matrices, and (though JSON has no tuple type) it would be nice to have them all on the same line instead of broken down, which makes the file harder tor ead and larger.

So basically:

~~~py
[{"matrix": (1, 0, 0, 1, 181, 0)}, {"matrix": (1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0)}]
~~~

would become:

~~~py
[
{
"matrix": [1, 0, 0, 1, 181, 0]
},
{
"matrix": [1, 0, 0, 1, 181, 0]
}
]
~~~

instead of:

~~~py
[
{
"matrix": [
1,
0,
0,
1,
181,
0
]
},
{
"matrix": [
1,
0,
0,
1,
181,
0
]
}
]
~~~

because tuples wouldn't get flattened.

Does that sound reasonable / is it feasible?

issue_title: Use widgets for output in jupyterlab kernel without a notebook
issue_body:
In [thebelab](https://github.com/minrk/thebelab), I'm using the JupyterLab kernel and output-area implementations. I'd like to be able to have widgets work *if available*, by adding widgets via additional script tags. It seems that I want something similar to the `html-manager` package, but that emits the RenderMime renderer (and possibly the WidgetManager, I'm not sure).

In particular, things I do not have on the page:

- JupyterLab, DocManager, etc.
- Notebook Documents

and it looks like the widget manager and extension assume these are in use. It also appears that there isn't a script-tag-embeddable form of anything other than html-manager. Is that right?

Ideally, what I'd like people to be able to do should look like:

```html
  <script type="text/javascript" src="https://unpkg.com/@jupyter-widgets/jupyterlab-manager@0.35"></script>
  <script type="text/javascript" src="https://unpkg.com/my-custom-widget@1.0" ></script>
  <script type="text/javascript" src="https://unpkg.com/thebelab@^0.2.0" ></script>
```

and then their custom widget would be available. If they need a little code to explicitly register custom widgets with the registry, that would be okay, too, but what is a critical requirement is that folks *do not* need to run a webpack build for thebelab with every possible combination of widgets.

Can I accomplish this now, or do I need some changes here before I can get started supporting widgets?

Or would it perhaps be more feasible if I include the WidgetManager in the thebelab bundle, and I confine the no-rebuild requirement to only support for *custom* widgets via additional tags?

issue_title: Investigate a Markov Random Field approach to clustering
issue_body:
After blocking and scoring, we get an array of predicted probabilities for pairs of records. We then need some way of combining these pair-wise probabilities into groups of records that we believe all refer to the same entity.

Right now, we take a two stage approach to this

1. first we treat these pairwise scores as an edgelist and find the connected components 
2. for each connected components we use hierarchical clustering for further partitioning.

The hierarchical clustering approach has two main problems. The first is mainly theoretic: hierarchical clustering really assumes that there is a metric distance between points and probabilities are not a metric distance. 

The second, and much more serious, problem is that this form of clustering requires a fully defined distance matrix. But, the connected components are usually not fully dense networks. Now, when we create the distance matrix, we treat missing edges as having no probability of making a match. This definitely not the right solution.

So, there are two ways I can think of doing this better.

1. we can make the connected components fully dense. We are investigating this in https://github.com/dedupeio/dedupe/pull/552 

- **pro** This resolves the problem of handling "missing" edges
- **con** a simple implementation can lead to massive networks which can obviate the benefits of blocking in the first place
- **con** a complicated implementation is... complicated and I'm not sure what the right way to do this is.

2. we can try a markov random field approach looking at this as a [Potts Model](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Potts_model) 

@bbengfort, @rebeccabilbro, @mattandahalfew, I think this could be a fun one to play with.

# This is a proposal and RFC for a new release for ONNX.

- Release Version: 1.0.1
- Release Type: Patch Release (backwards compatible bug fixes only) 
(following Semantic Versioning as defined in [semver.org](https://semver.org/))
- Release Manager: @lupesko
- Release Checklist:
  - [ ] Create a new branch from v1.0, named v1.0.1
  - [ ] Cherry pick the approved commits
  - [ ] Ensure all tests are successful, work with the community to address any failing tests
  - [ ] Use GitHub Release feature to tag ("v1.0.1") and publish the release
  - [ ] Release Description: "Backwards compatible bug fixes only on top of v1.0" and details of the commits included
  - [ ] Build and upload a new PyPi package ONNX 1.0.1
- Release Content:
  - Will be managed via GitHub Milestones: we will create a new Milestone: v1.0.1 Release, and tag all issues and PRs for the release with the Milestone
  - Initial proposal of commits:
    - #430 (issue #432) - PyPi install fails when git is not installed

**Please share your feedback and suggestions for the release**
**Note that a patch release is intended to include bug fixes only, and be backward compatible.**

Currently the function [`OgcRequest.get_dates`](http://sentinelhub-py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/data_request.html#sentinelhub.data_request.OgcRequest.get_dates) queries dates for which L1C products are available. It does so regardless of whether source in [Sentinel Hub Configuration Utility](https://apps.sentinel-hub.com/configurator/#/) is set to `L1C` or  something else (such as `L2A`).

Although there is a plan to have an `L2A` for each `L1C` product, we currently provide few `L2A` products. Consequently, when one has source set to `L2A`  in the Configuration Utility, some queries may result in empty images (see issue #20 ).

We should fix this in the upcoming releases.

We can fix the pypi metadata or better yet upload it to pypi.

Thanks Eikeon! I've uploaded this to pypi and removed the old link. I've also uploaded it here on google code.

issue_title: Graph().parse() and relative URIs and blank nodes
issue_body:
danielmr...@gmail.com, 2011-05-16T09:41:20.000Z 
What steps will reproduce the problem?

1. Parse xml from string and print results:

g1=rdflib.graph.ConjunctiveGraph()
    g1.parse(data="""


 
  
 


 
  
 

""")

    for s,p,o in g1:
        print s,p,o

2. Parse the same xml from url:    

    g2=rdflib.graph.ConjunctiveGraph()
    g2.parse("http://127.0.0.1:5000/test")

    for s,p,o in g2:
        print s,p,o


What is the expected output? What do you see instead?

I expect the output to be the same but the xml string does not contain a URI to attach the relative URIs to.

Result of the string parsing:

#John http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/knows owiEBRcs5
#John http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/Person

#Mary http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/Person
owiEBRcs5 http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/Person

Result of the uri parsing:

http://127.0.0.1:5000/test#John http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/knows owiEBRcs5
http://127.0.0.1:5000/test#John http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/Person
http://127.0.0.1:5000/test#Mary http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/knows owiEBRcs5
http://127.0.0.1:5000/test#Mary http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/Person
owiEBRcs5 http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#type http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/Person


What version of the product are you using? On what operating system?
rdflib trunk with python 2.6


I would like to add an option to g.parse() that allows to define a URI to add as a prefix for relative URIs.
Also it would be helpful to have a possibility to add a real URI to blank nodes, too.
What else does parse() need?

Addition to the initial post here: one could pass the "publicID" -->
g.parse(data=data, publicID="http://127.0.0.1:5000/test")

issue_title: Allow specification of fields in Search
issue_body:
The search class is a very helpful thing to find id's of what you're looking for, but I often feel like searching for just a string is a handicap. Allowing the specification of fields would make searching a lot more powerful.

For example:

```python
search = Search('baby one more time', artist = 'britney spears')
```

The above Search object could return albums that feature a song with the name of the first argument or an album, single, EP or whatever with that name, while keeping the artist limitation (which might be a search in itself) in mind.
